title: Optimization with Optuna: A Practical and Theoretical Guide to Modern Black-Box Optimization
text:
Preface
This book is a practical and theoretical guide to using Optuna as a modern black-box optimization framework. It is written for readers who already know Python, machine learning, and standard hyperparameter-search ideas, but want a deeper and more systematic understanding of how Optuna works and where it fits beyond routine model tuning.
Most introductions to Optuna stop at the mechanics of create_study, trial.suggest_*, and study.optimize. Those tools matter, but they are only the surface. The more important questions are structural: what kind of optimization problem are you actually solving, why do some search spaces fail, when does TPE outperform simpler baselines, how should pruning be interpreted, and what changes when optimization becomes distributed, persistent, and production-facing.
This book is organized as a progression from foundations to applications. The early chapters explain the optimization setting Optuna was built for. The middle chapters cover search-space design, samplers, pruning, diagnostics, and distributed execution. The later chapters apply those ideas to machine learning, general black-box optimization, and production workflows, before ending with a capstone portfolio in Final Project.
The intended reading style is hands-on but critical. Most chapters include runnable examples, but the goal is not only to run code successfully. The goal is to understand why a workflow is well designed, when a result should be trusted, and what trade-offs appear once optimization is used in realistic systems.

title: Optimization with Optuna: A Practical and Theoretical Guide to Modern Black-Box Optimization
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book assumes that you already have:

working knowledge of Python
familiarity with machine-learning evaluation
basic understanding of grid search and random search
enough mathematical maturity to follow optimization arguments and probability-based intuition

It is not written as a first introduction to machine learning or Python. It is written for practitioners who want to move from “I can tune a model with Optuna” to “I can design and justify optimization workflows across several problem classes.”

title: Optimization with Optuna: A Practical and Theoretical Guide to Modern Black-Box Optimization
text:
What You Will Learn
By the end of the book, you should be able to:

explain the black-box optimization setting that motivates Optuna
understand the role of Bayesian optimization ideas and the TPE algorithm
design effective search spaces, including conditional and hierarchical ones
choose and interpret samplers and pruners more deliberately
diagnose optimization behavior using visual and statistical signals
scale studies across persistent and distributed workflows
apply Optuna to machine learning, benchmark optimization, and simulation-based objectives
structure optimization work so that results are reproducible and operationally credible

title: Optimization with Optuna: A Practical and Theoretical Guide to Modern Black-Box Optimization
text:
How To Use The Book
There are two reasonable ways to read it.

Read it sequentially if you want the full conceptual arc from foundations to capstone.
Read by need if you already use Optuna and want targeted guidance on topics such as Designing Search Spaces, Visualization and Diagnostics, or Production Workflows.

If you plan to implement serious optimization workflows, the strongest path is sequential reading followed by the capstone chapter. The chapters are designed to build on one another, especially from the TPE discussion onward.

title: Optimization with Optuna: A Practical and Theoretical Guide to Modern Black-Box Optimization
text:
A Note On Scope
Optuna is often introduced as a hyperparameter optimization library, but this book deliberately uses a wider framing. Hyperparameter tuning is one major application, not the whole story. The same Optuna abstractions can be used for simulation calibration, benchmark-function optimization, algorithm configuration, and other black-box search problems where evaluation is expensive or noisy.
That broader perspective is the organizing idea of the book.

title: Introduction
text:
The Problem Optuna Solves
Optuna is one of the most effective libraries available for modern black-box optimization in Python. Many users encounter it through hyperparameter tuning, but that entry point can hide its deeper value. Optuna is not merely a convenience wrapper around search loops. It is a flexible framework for making optimization decisions under limited budgets, expensive evaluations, mixed search spaces, and uncertain objective behavior.
This book takes that broader view seriously. Its central claim is that Optuna becomes far more useful once you understand the optimization setting it was designed for, the statistical logic behind its default sampler, and the engineering discipline required to evaluate and operationalize the results.
The class of problems Optuna handles well has a recognizable structure.
You have a function or system that can be evaluated, but not easily analyzed. Each evaluation may be expensive, noisy, or stochastic. The search space may include continuous, integer, categorical, or conditional decisions. Gradients may be unavailable or irrelevant. And the total trial budget may be small enough that brute-force search is wasteful.
That is the black-box optimization setting. In practical terms, it includes problems such as:
Optuna gives you a way to search these spaces adaptively rather than blindly.

title: Introduction
text:
Why This Book Exists
There are many code examples online that show how to use Optuna, but fewer resources explain the reasoning behind effective usage. In practice, mistakes often happen at a higher level than syntax.
Common failures include:

badly designed search spaces
overconfident interpretation of noisy results
leakage in machine-learning evaluation
misuse of persistent storage or distributed workers
premature confidence in one sampler without understanding the landscape

This book is meant to close that gap. It combines theory, implementation, and workflow design so that Optuna is treated as part of an optimization methodology, not just a library call.

title: Introduction
text:
How The Book Is Organized
The book is divided into four parts.

Part I: Foundations
The opening modules explain what black-box optimization is, why classical exhaustive search fails, how Bayesian optimization is framed conceptually, and why TPE matters so much in Optuna’s design.


Part II: Core Usage
The next part covers the mechanics that determine whether Optuna is effective in practice: studies, trials, parameter suggestion APIs, search-space design, sampler behavior, and pruning.


Part III: Advanced Techniques
This part moves into multi-objective optimization, diagnostics, distributed execution, and the deeper operational patterns required once studies become more complex or costly.


Part IV: Applications
The final part applies the earlier ideas to machine learning, general black-box optimization, production workflows, and a capstone portfolio in Final Project.
The structure is intentionally cumulative. Later chapters assume you understand not just the API, but the optimization logic behind it.

title: Introduction
section: What You Should Expect From The Examples
text:
What You Should Expect From The Examples
Most important chapters include runnable examples, but the examples are intentionally small. The goal is to make the logic visible rather than hide it in a large notebook or application scaffold.
You should expect examples that are:

short enough to read in one sitting
self-contained when practical
designed to illustrate one main idea at a time
connected to realistic trade-offs rather than only happy-path usage

Where a code block is not meant to execute during the book build, it is presented as a copyable pattern rather than as a runnable notebook cell. That distinction is deliberate. The book tries to balance correctness, readability, and real workflow relevance.

title: Introduction
text:
A Working Mental Model
One useful way to think about Optuna is as a coordination layer around repeated experiments.
define search space
    -> evaluate candidate
    -> record result
    -> update search strategy
    -> repeat under budget
Everything else in the book refines that loop.

The theory chapters explain why the loop should be adaptive.
The architecture chapters explain how Optuna represents the loop internally.
The search-space and sampler chapters explain how to make the loop effective.
The diagnostics and production chapters explain how to make the loop trustworthy.

title: Introduction
text:
What “Mastery” Looks Like Here
By the end of the book, mastery does not mean memorizing every API. It means being able to answer questions such as:

Is this a good optimization problem for Optuna?
Is the search space well designed for the actual decision structure?
Does the evaluation protocol measure what we claim it measures?
Are the results robust enough to trust?
Which sampler or pruning strategy is justified here?
Can this workflow be resumed, inspected, and operationalized later?

That is the standard this book is written toward.

title: Introduction
text:
Suggested Reading Path
If you are new to serious optimization work, read the book in order. If you already use Optuna, you can still benefit from the following path:

Start with the foundations to align the optimization framing.
Read the chapters on search spaces, samplers, and pruning carefully.
Use the diagnostics and production chapters to harden your workflows.
Finish with the capstone in Final Project.

The next chapter, Foundations of Modern Optimization, starts with the fundamental question behind everything that follows: what kind of optimization problem are we really trying to solve when we reach for a tool like Optuna?

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.1 Why This Matters
This chapter establishes why modern optimization matters for Optuna users. Once objective evaluations become expensive, noisy, high-dimensional, or non-differentiable, the search strategy matters as much as the model itself.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to explain what optimization problem Optuna is designed to solve, why naive search strategies break down, and why Bayesian optimization is a practical response.
Suppose you are tuning a machine learning pipeline with a learning rate, tree depth, regularization strength, and feature preprocessing choices. Even before selecting a model, you already face a search problem over a mixed space of continuous, discrete, and categorical variables.
That is the setting where Optuna becomes useful: not ordinary calculus-based optimization, but efficient search over expensive black-box objectives.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.2 Formal Definition of Optimization
In mathematical terms, optimization is the process of finding the best input parameters for a function.
Typically, we want to solve:
\[
x^* = \arg\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} f(x)
\]
where:

\(f(x)\) is the objective function.
\(x\) is the parameter vector.
\(\mathcal{X}\) is the search space.
\(x^*\) is the optimal parameter setting.

The problem can also be written as a maximization task:
\[
x^* = \arg\max_{x \in \mathcal{X}} f(x)
\]

1.2.1 Example
Consider the problem of minimizing:
\[
f(x) = (x - 2)^2
\]
The optimal solution is:
\[
x^* = 2
\]
This is a simple convex function. In realistic problems, however, the objective may be non-convex, noisy, or expensive to evaluate.
In machine learning, for example, we often optimize an objective such as:
\[
f(x) = \text{validation error of a model}
\]
where \(x\) represents hyperparameters.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.3 Black-Box Optimization
Many real-world optimization problems are black-box functions. In these cases:

We cannot compute gradients.
We do not know the analytical form of the objective.
We can only evaluate the function at selected points.

Operationally, this means we can query \(f(x)\), observe the result, and use that observation to decide what to try next.
Examples include:



Problem
Why it is black box




ML training
Complex training dynamics


Simulations
Physics or system engines


A/B experiments
Real-world user behavior


Monte Carlo simulations
Stochastic outputs



In these settings, traditional methods such as gradient descent, Newton methods, or closed-form optimization are often unavailable. Instead, we rely on sampling-based optimization.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.4 Curse of Dimensionality
Search spaces grow exponentially with the number of parameters.
If each parameter has \(k\) possible values and we have \(d\) parameters, the total number of combinations is:
\[
k^d
\]
For example:



Parameters
Values each
Total combinations




5
10
100,000


10
10
10,000,000,000



This makes exhaustive search infeasible very quickly. The consequence is not just that full search becomes slow, but that most of the space is never explored at all.
Important consequences:

Sampling efficiency becomes critical.
Intelligent search strategies matter more than brute force.
A poor search space definition can dominate the final result.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.5 Grid Search vs Random Search

1.5.1 Grid Search
Grid search evaluates every combination in a predefined grid.
Example:



learning_rate
depth




0.01
3


0.01
6


0.1
3


0.1
6



Advantages:

Simple.
Deterministic.
Easy to parallelize.

Disadvantages:

Scales exponentially.
Becomes inefficient in high dimensions.
Wastes evaluations on unimportant parameters.



1.5.2 Random Search
Random search samples parameters independently from the search space.
x1 = random()
x2 = random()
x3 = random()
...
The key practical insight is that random search often outperforms grid search when only a subset of parameters strongly affects performance. Grid search spends many evaluations covering dimensions that may not matter much, while random search covers more distinct combinations of the important ones.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.6 Sample Efficiency
When evaluations are cheap, brute-force methods can be acceptable. But many real objectives are expensive:



Task
Evaluation cost




ML training
Minutes


Deep learning
Hours


Simulation
Minutes


Scientific experiment
Days



If we can only afford a small budget, each evaluation should be as informative as possible. This is the idea of sample efficiency: finding strong solutions with as few objective evaluations as possible.
Random search does not use the results of earlier trials. Modern optimizers do.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.7 Exploration vs Exploitation
Every optimizer must balance two competing goals.

1.7.1 Exploration
Explore unknown regions of the search space to discover potentially better areas.


1.7.2 Exploitation
Search near known good solutions to refine promising regions.
Too much exploration slows convergence. Too much exploitation can trap the search in a local optimum. Practical optimization algorithms succeed by balancing these two behaviors dynamically.





flowchart LR
    A[Search space] --> B[Evaluate candidate]
    B --> C{Result promising?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Exploit nearby region]
    C -->|No| E[Explore elsewhere]
    D --> F[Update search strategy]
    E --> F
    F --> B

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.8 When Gradient-Based Methods Fail
Gradient-based methods work well when the objective is differentiable, gradients are available, and the optimization landscape is well behaved. Those assumptions fail in many real applications.



Problem
Why gradients fail




Discrete parameters
Gradients are undefined


Simulation-based models
Gradients are unavailable


Non-differentiable functions
Discontinuities


Noisy objectives
Gradients become unstable



Examples of non-gradient-friendly choices include:

Number of trees.
Optimizer type.
Activation function.

These are categorical or discrete decisions, so gradient descent is not the right tool.






Common Mistake



Treating hyperparameter tuning as if the objective were smooth, convex, and differentiable leads to the wrong optimization strategy. In Optuna-style workloads, the objective is often mixed, noisy, and black box by construction.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.9 Why Bayesian Optimization Works
Bayesian optimization is designed for expensive black-box functions. Instead of optimizing the true objective directly, it builds a surrogate model:
\[
\hat{f}(x)
\]
The surrogate approximates the true objective using past observations. An acquisition function then decides which point should be evaluated next.
At a high level, the process is:

Evaluate several candidate points.
Fit a probabilistic model from the observed results.
Estimate which regions look promising or uncertain.
Evaluate the next candidate selected by the acquisition rule.
Update the model.
Repeat.

Advantages:

Uses information from previous trials.
Balances exploration and exploitation.
Reaches strong solutions in fewer evaluations.

That is why Bayesian optimization is widely used in hyperparameter tuning, robotics, engineering design, and reinforcement learning.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.10 Where Optuna Fits
Optuna implements a modern Bayesian optimization workflow, with TPE (Tree-structured Parzen Estimator) as its default sampler.
Compared with Gaussian-process-based approaches, TPE is especially practical because it:

Scales better to higher dimensions.
Handles categorical variables naturally.
Works well with conditional search spaces.

We will study this algorithm in more detail in The TPE Algorithm.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.11 Practical Exercise: Implement Random Search
As a first exercise, implement a simple optimizer for the function:
\[
f(x) = (x - 3)^2 + \sin(5x)
\]

import math
import random


def objective(x):
    return (x - 3) ** 2 + math.sin(5 * x)


best_x = None
best_y = float("inf")

for _ in range(1000):
    x = random.uniform(-5, 5)
    y = objective(x)

    if y < best_y:
        best_y = y
        best_x = x

print(f"Best x: {best_x:.4f}")
print(f"Best value: {best_y:.4f}")

Best x: 3.4066
Best value: -0.8046


This optimizer does three things:

Samples random points.
Evaluates the objective.
Keeps the best solution found so far.

Its limitations are exactly what motivate Optuna: it ignores previous evaluations and does not adapt to what it learns.
Try extending the exercise in three steps:

Reduce the evaluation budget from 1000 to 50 and compare the result.
Change the search interval and observe how sensitive random search is to space design.
Rewrite the objective in two dimensions and see how quickly brute-force search degrades.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.12 Recap
Modern optimization in machine learning and data science often involves:

Expensive black-box functions.
High-dimensional search spaces.
Discrete or non-differentiable parameters.

That combination makes classical exhaustive or gradient-based methods a poor default. Bayesian optimization improves sample efficiency by learning from prior evaluations, and Optuna is designed to make that workflow practical.

title: 1  Foundations of Modern Optimization
text:
1.13 Decision Guide
Use the following rule of thumb:

Use grid search only for very small, fully discrete spaces.
Use random search as a strong baseline when evaluations are cheap.
Use Bayesian optimization when evaluations are expensive and each trial should teach the optimizer something.

Next, we will make those ideas precise in Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.1 Why This Matters
This chapter introduces the core logic behind Bayesian optimization: build a probabilistic model of an expensive objective, estimate where information is most valuable, and decide where to evaluate next. That is the conceptual bridge between the search intuition in Foundations of Modern Optimization and Optuna’s default TPE-based workflow.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to explain the role of surrogate models, why acquisition functions are needed, and why classical Gaussian-process-based Bayesian optimization is both powerful and limited.
This chapter assumes you are already comfortable with the optimization vocabulary from Foundations of Modern Optimization, along with basic probability concepts such as mean, variance, and normal distributions.
Suppose each hyperparameter trial for a large model takes two hours. You cannot afford to evaluate hundreds or thousands of random configurations just to discover where the promising region might be. You need a strategy that treats each evaluation as expensive and uses prior results to make better decisions.
That is the problem Bayesian optimization is built to solve.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.3 Why Surrogate Models Matter
Bayesian optimization starts from a simple idea: instead of optimizing the true objective function directly, we build an approximate probabilistic model from the observations we already have.
Let the true objective be:
\[
f(x)
\]
We build a surrogate model:
\[
\hat{f}(x)
\]
using observed data:
\[
D = \{(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)\}
\]
where:
\[
y_i = f(x_i)
\]
The purpose of the surrogate is not just to estimate the function value. It must also estimate uncertainty. In other words, for each candidate point \(x\), we want a predictive distribution:
\[
p(f(x) \mid D)
\]
That uncertainty estimate is what makes exploration possible.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.4 Gaussian Processes: The Classical View
The most classical surrogate model in Bayesian optimization is the Gaussian process, usually abbreviated GP.
A Gaussian process defines a distribution over functions rather than a single best-fit curve. Formally:
\[
f(x) \sim \mathcal{GP}(m(x), k(x, x'))
\]
where:

\(m(x)\) is the mean function.
\(k(x, x')\) is the covariance kernel.

The mean function captures the expected value of the objective, and the kernel encodes similarity between inputs. If two inputs are close according to the kernel, the GP expects their outputs to be similar as well.
Common kernels include:

Radial basis function (RBF).
Matern.
Rational quadratic.

The intuition is more important than the formulas at this stage: a Gaussian process gives you both a prediction and a measure of confidence in that prediction.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.5 What Does a GP Predict?
At a candidate point \(x\), a GP provides:

A predictive mean:

\[
\mu(x)
\]

A predictive variance:

\[
\sigma^2(x)
\]
So the prediction can be summarized as:
\[
f(x) \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \sigma^2(x))
\]
This is the core output that drives Bayesian optimization. The optimizer now has two pieces of information:

What value looks plausible at this point.
How uncertain that estimate is.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
section: 2.6 How the Acquisition Function Chooses the Next Point
text:
2.6 How the Acquisition Function Chooses the Next Point
The surrogate model tells us what we currently believe about the objective. The acquisition function tells us what to do next.
In general, the next evaluation point is chosen by solving:
\[
x_{\text{next}} = \arg\max a(x)
\]
where \(a(x)\) is the acquisition function.
Acquisition functions encode the exploration-exploitation tradeoff. They prefer points that either:

Look promising according to the predicted mean.
Remain uncertain enough that evaluating them could reveal something better.






flowchart LR
    A[Observed trials] --> B[Fit surrogate model]
    B --> C[Estimate mean and uncertainty]
    C --> D[Compute acquisition score]
    D --> E[Choose next point]
    E --> F[Evaluate objective]
    F --> A

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.7 Probability of Improvement
Probability of Improvement, or PI, is the simplest acquisition function.
For minimization, let \(f^*\) denote the best observed value so far. Then PI measures the probability that a candidate point beats that value:
\[
PI(x) = P(f(x) < f^*)
\]
Its interpretation is straightforward: rank points by how likely they are to improve the current best.
The limitation is equally straightforward: PI often over-exploits. A point that is only marginally better but very certain can dominate a riskier point that could lead to a much larger improvement.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.8 Expected Improvement
Expected Improvement, or EI, fixes the main weakness of PI by asking not only whether improvement is likely, but how large that improvement might be.
For minimization:
\[
EI(x) = \mathbb{E}[\max(f^* - f(x), 0)]
\]
This combines:

Low predicted values.
High uncertainty.

That balance is why EI became one of the standard acquisition rules in Bayesian optimization.
The fast example at the start of the chapter is an explicit EI calculation, and it shows the core idea: the next point is chosen for decision value, not just predicted objective value.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.9 Upper and Lower Confidence Bounds
Another family of acquisition functions uses confidence bounds directly.
For maximization:
\[
UCB(x) = \mu(x) + \kappa \sigma(x)
\]
For minimization, the analogous lower confidence bound is:
\[
LCB(x) = \mu(x) - \kappa \sigma(x)
\]
where:

\(\mu(x)\) is the predicted mean.
\(\sigma(x)\) is the predictive standard deviation.
\(\kappa\) controls how aggressively the optimizer explores.

Large \(\kappa\) favors exploration. Small \(\kappa\) favors exploitation. This makes confidence-bound strategies attractive when you want a direct knob for controlling search behavior.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.10 The Bayesian Optimization Loop
Putting the pieces together, the classical Bayesian optimization loop is:

Sample a small set of initial points.
Fit a surrogate model such as a Gaussian process.
Compute an acquisition function over the search space.
Select the point that maximizes the acquisition score.
Evaluate the true objective.
Update the model with the new observation.
Repeat.

This loop works because the optimizer spends evaluations where they are expected to be most informative, not merely where the search space happens to be easy to enumerate.






Common Mistake



Do not confuse optimizing the surrogate mean with performing Bayesian optimization. If you always choose the point with the best predicted mean, you remove the exploration term and the method can get trapped in regions that only look good under the current model.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.11 Where Gaussian-Process Bayesian Optimization Struggles
Gaussian-process-based Bayesian optimization is elegant, but it has practical limits.

2.11.1 Computational Complexity
Training a Gaussian process typically scales as:
\[
O(n^3)
\]
where \(n\) is the number of observations. That becomes expensive once the number of evaluations grows.


2.11.2 High-Dimensional Search Spaces
Gaussian processes usually work best in relatively low-dimensional settings. Once the search space contains many interacting hyperparameters, the model quality and acquisition optimization can degrade quickly.
As a rough rule of thumb, performance often becomes harder to maintain once the effective search dimension grows well beyond the small-scale regime.


2.11.3 Categorical and Conditional Variables
Many machine learning search spaces include categorical or tree-structured choices such as:
optimizer = ["adam", "sgd", "rmsprop"]
This is awkward for classical GP kernels, which are more naturally defined over continuous spaces.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
section: 2.12 Why Optuna Transitions to TPE Instead
text:
2.12 Why Optuna Transitions to TPE Instead
Optuna does not use Gaussian processes as its default strategy. Instead, it uses the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator, or TPE.
The conceptual shift is important. Classical Bayesian optimization often models:
\[
p(y \mid x)
\]
TPE instead models:
\[
p(x \mid y)
\]
It separates the observed trials into good and bad regions, estimates densities for each group, and searches for points that are more likely under the good-trial density than under the bad-trial density.
This leads to an optimization criterion of the form:
\[
\arg\max \frac{\ell(x)}{g(x)}
\]
where:

\(\ell(x)\) is the density of good observations.
\(g(x)\) is the density of bad observations.

This reformulation is one reason TPE is practical for Optuna. It handles categorical variables better, scales more gracefully, and works well in higher-dimensional or conditional search spaces.
We will unpack the full algorithm in The TPE Algorithm.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
section: 2.13 Practical Exercise: Compare Mean and Improvement
text:
2.13 Practical Exercise: Compare Mean and Improvement
Use the earlier Expected Improvement example as a starting point and modify it in three steps:

Change f_best and observe how the acquisition ranking changes.
Increase the uncertainty of one candidate and check when exploration becomes dominant.
Add a new candidate with a very low mean and very low uncertainty, then compare it against a riskier candidate with higher uncertainty.

Your goal is to build intuition for why acquisition functions do not behave like plain objective minimization.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.14 Recap
Bayesian optimization combines three components:

A surrogate model that approximates the unknown objective.
An uncertainty estimate that makes exploration possible.
An acquisition function that selects the next evaluation point.

Gaussian processes provide the classical picture of this workflow, and they remain useful as a conceptual foundation. But the same chapter also explains why Optuna’s default strategy moves away from GP-based optimization in practice.

title: 2  Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals
text:
2.15 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Use Bayesian optimization when each objective evaluation is expensive enough that learning from earlier trials matters.
Use Gaussian-process intuition to understand surrogate modeling and acquisition functions.
Expect GP-based methods to struggle once the space becomes highly dimensional, categorical, or heavily conditional.
Expect Optuna’s TPE-based approach to be a better practical default for many real hyperparameter-search workloads.

Next, The TPE Algorithm turns that transition into a concrete optimization method.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.1 Why This Matters
This chapter explains the algorithm Optuna uses by default: the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator, or TPE. Instead of fitting a regression model for the objective as in classical Gaussian-process Bayesian optimization, TPE models densities over parameter values conditioned on whether past trials were good or bad.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to explain why TPE models \(p(x \mid y)\) rather than \(p(y \mid x)\), how the good-trial and bad-trial densities are constructed, and why that design works well for Optuna’s flexible search spaces.
This chapter assumes you already understand surrogate models and acquisition functions from Bayesian Optimization Fundamentals.
Suppose you are tuning a real machine-learning pipeline with continuous hyperparameters, categorical choices, and conditional branches such as model-specific parameters. A Gaussian-process surrogate becomes awkward quickly, both computationally and structurally.
TPE matters because it keeps the central idea of Bayesian optimization, learning from prior trials, while replacing the classical GP machinery with something that works much better in the messy search spaces common in Optuna workloads.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.2 A Fast First Win
Before looking at the full algorithm, it helps to see the core ranking rule in action. TPE divides past observations into good and bad trials, estimates a density for each group, and then scores candidates by how much more they resemble good trials than bad ones.
The following pure-Python example implements a tiny one-dimensional TPE-like ranking loop using Gaussian kernels. It is not a production implementation, but it captures the mechanism that matters conceptually.

import math


def objective(x):
    return (x - 2.0) ** 2 + math.sin(3.0 * x)


def quantile(values, q):
    ordered = sorted(values)
    index = int(q * (len(ordered) - 1))
    return ordered[index]


def gaussian_kernel(x, center, bandwidth):
    z = (x - center) / bandwidth
    return math.exp(-0.5 * z * z) / (bandwidth * math.sqrt(2.0 * math.pi))


def kde(points, x, bandwidth=0.35):
    if not points:
        return 1e-12
    return sum(gaussian_kernel(x, point, bandwidth) for point in points) / len(points)


observations = []
for x in [-4.0, -2.5, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 1.8, 2.4, 3.2, 4.0]:
    observations.append((x, objective(x)))

gamma = 0.3
threshold = quantile([y for _, y in observations], gamma)
good = [x for x, y in observations if y < threshold]
bad = [x for x, y in observations if y >= threshold]

candidates = [round(-4.0 + 0.25 * i, 2) for i in range(33)]
scored = []

for x in candidates:
    l_x = kde(good, x)
    g_x = kde(bad, x)
    ratio = l_x / max(g_x, 1e-12)
    scored.append((ratio, x, l_x, g_x))

best_ratio, best_x, _, _ = max(scored)

print("Good trials:", good)
print("Bad trials:", bad)
print("Suggested next x:", best_x)
print("Best density ratio:", round(best_ratio, 4))

Good trials: [1.8, 2.4]
Bad trials: [-4.0, -2.5, -1.0, 0.0, 1.0, 3.2, 4.0]
Suggested next x: 2.0
Best density ratio: 243.5948


The important point is not the exact numerical value of the ratio. The point is that TPE selects candidates that look statistically similar to good trials while remaining dissimilar to bad ones.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.3 Why TPE Flips the Bayesian Optimization View
text:
3.3 Why TPE Flips the Bayesian Optimization View
Classical Bayesian optimization typically models:
\[
p(y \mid x)
\]
That asks: given a parameter setting \(x\), what objective value \(y\) should we expect?
TPE flips the problem and models:
\[
p(x \mid y)
\]
That asks: which parameter values tend to be associated with good or bad objective values?
This is a major conceptual shift. Instead of learning a full regression model for the objective surface, TPE turns optimization into a density-estimation problem.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.4 Splitting Observations into Good and Bad Trials
text:
3.4 Splitting Observations into Good and Bad Trials
Assume we have already evaluated \(n\) trials:
\[
(x_1, y_1), (x_2, y_2), \ldots, (x_n, y_n)
\]
TPE defines a threshold \(y^*\) such that the best fraction of observations is treated as the good region. If we denote that fraction by \(\gamma\), then the good set is usually the best \(10\%\) to \(25\%\) of trials.
Typical values are:
\[
\gamma \approx 0.1 \text{ to } 0.25
\]
For a minimization problem, we then split the trials into:

Good observations, where \(y < y^*\).
Bad observations, where \(y \ge y^*\).

That split is what allows TPE to build two separate models of the search space.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.5 The Two Density Models
TPE estimates two densities:
\[
\ell(x) = p(x \mid y < y^*)
\]
\[
g(x) = p(x \mid y \ge y^*)
\]
Interpretation:



Density
Meaning




\(\ell(x)\)
Parameter values associated with good objective values


\(g(x)\)
Parameter values associated with worse objective values



The optimizer then searches for candidates that score well under the good density and poorly under the bad density.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.6 The Likelihood-Ratio Rule
The next candidate is chosen by maximizing a density ratio of the form:
\[
\frac{\ell(x)}{g(x)}
\]
This plays a role similar to an acquisition function in classical Bayesian optimization.
Candidates with a high ratio are appealing because they:

Resemble parameter settings that produced strong results.
Avoid regions already associated with weak results.

This is the core decision rule behind TPE.





flowchart LR
    A[Observed trials] --> B[Split by quantile threshold]
    B --> C[Good trials]
    B --> D[Bad trials]
    C --> E["Estimate l(x)"]
    D --> F["Estimate g(x)"]
    E --> G[Sample candidates]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Choose candidate with high l(x) / g(x)"]
    H --> I[Evaluate objective]
    I --> A

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.7 How Parzen Estimators Build the Densities
text:
3.7 How Parzen Estimators Build the Densities
The density estimates in TPE are typically constructed using Parzen estimators, which are a form of kernel density estimation.
Given samples \(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\), a kernel density estimate can be written as:
\[
p(x) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K(x - x_i)
\]
where \(K\) is a kernel function, often Gaussian in simple explanations.
The intuition is straightforward: each observed value contributes a smooth local bump, and the sum of those bumps forms a continuous approximation of the density.
In practice, TPE applies this idea parameter by parameter and uses a design that works well with continuous, discrete, and categorical search spaces.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.8 Where Exploration Comes From in TPE
text:
3.8 Where Exploration Comes From in TPE
TPE still balances exploration and exploitation, but it does so differently from GP-based acquisition functions.

3.8.1 Density Ratio
The ratio
\[
\frac{\ell(x)}{g(x)}
\]
pushes the search toward promising regions.


3.8.2 Candidate Sampling
TPE does not optimize the ratio analytically over the full search space. Instead, it samples many candidates and ranks them. That sampling process preserves some exploration even when the model has strong preferences.


3.8.3 Quantile Threshold
The value of \(\gamma\) controls how selective the good set is. A lower \(\gamma\) makes the good set smaller and more exploitative. A higher \(\gamma\) broadens the good set and tends to encourage more exploration.
A typical value is:
\[
\gamma = 0.15
\]

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.9 The Full TPE Procedure
A simplified TPE workflow looks like this:

Sample an initial set of random trials.
Evaluate the objective.
Split the observed trials into good and bad sets.
Estimate \(\ell(x)\) and \(g(x)\).
Sample candidate points from the promising region.
Score candidates using \(\ell(x) / g(x)\).
Evaluate the best-scoring candidate.
Update the observed trial set.
Repeat.

The crucial detail is that TPE samples many candidates and chooses among them, rather than solving a closed-form acquisition optimization problem.






Common Mistake



Do not interpret TPE as directly estimating the optimum or directly predicting the objective value. TPE is ranking candidates through density comparison. That is a different kind of model from GP regression, and misunderstanding that difference often leads to confused explanations of how Optuna’s sampler works.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.10 Why TPE Works Well in Practice
text:
3.10 Why TPE Works Well in Practice
TPE has several practical advantages over Gaussian-process-based Bayesian optimization.

3.10.1 High-Dimensional Spaces
Gaussian processes tend to become harder to manage as the search space grows in dimension and interaction complexity. TPE is often much more robust in problems with dozens of hyperparameters.


3.10.2 Categorical Variables
TPE handles categorical parameters naturally, which is a major advantage in real hyperparameter tuning.
For example, an Optuna search space may contain a categorical branch such as:
optimizer = trial.suggest_categorical(
    "optimizer",
    ["adam", "sgd", "rmsprop"],
)


3.10.3 Tree-Structured Search Spaces
TPE is especially well suited for conditional search spaces, which is where the “tree-structured” part of its name becomes practical.
model = trial.suggest_categorical("model", ["xgboost", "svm"])

if model == "xgboost":
    depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 10)
This kind of conditional structure is common in Optuna studies and awkward for classical GP formulations.


3.10.4 Better Scalability
In broad terms, TPE tends to scale much more gracefully than Gaussian-process approaches, whose training cost can become prohibitive as the number of observations grows.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.11 Why Optuna Uses TPE by Default
text:
3.11 Why Optuna Uses TPE by Default
Optuna is designed around three practical goals:

Strong performance in hyperparameter optimization.
Flexible search spaces.
Scalability to realistic workloads.

TPE aligns well with all three, which is why Optuna uses it as the default sampler.
That does not mean TPE is the only valid strategy. Optuna also supports alternatives such as random search, CMA-ES, and NSGA-II, which we will revisit later in the book.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
section: 3.12 Practical Exercise: Implement a Simplified TPE Loop
text:
3.12 Practical Exercise: Implement a Simplified TPE Loop
Extend the fast example at the top of the chapter in four steps:

Add a loop so the newly suggested point is appended to the observed trials.
Recompute the good and bad split after each new evaluation.
Change the value of gamma and observe how the search behavior shifts.
Replace the candidate grid with random candidate sampling and compare the selected points.

The goal is not to reproduce Optuna’s production sampler exactly. The goal is to internalize the density-ratio idea that drives the algorithm.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.13 Recap
TPE reformulates Bayesian optimization as density estimation.
Instead of modeling:
\[
p(y \mid x)
\]
it models:
\[
p(x \mid y)
\]
This leads to two densities, \(\ell(x)\) and \(g(x)\), and a candidate-selection rule based on their ratio. That design is one reason Optuna can handle categorical variables, conditional spaces, and larger search problems more naturally than many GP-based approaches.

title: 3  The TPE Algorithm
text:
3.14 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Think of TPE as Bayesian optimization through density comparison rather than surrogate regression.
Use it when the search space includes categorical or conditional choices.
Expect it to be a strong default for practical Optuna studies.
Do not expect it to provide the same style of uncertainty model as a Gaussian process.

Next, Optuna Architecture connects this optimization logic to the concrete building blocks of an Optuna study: studies, trials, samplers, pruners, and storage.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.1 Why This Matters
This chapter explains how Optuna is structured internally and how its main components cooperate during optimization. Once you understand the architecture, the library becomes easier to reason about: a study manages trials, a sampler proposes parameters, a pruner decides whether a trial should continue, and storage records the whole process.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to describe the role of the study, trial, objective function, search space, sampler, pruner, and storage backend, and explain how they interact inside Optuna’s optimization loop.
This chapter assumes you already understand the optimization ideas behind The TPE Algorithm. Here the focus shifts from theory to the actual objects Optuna exposes.
Many Optuna users start by copying a short example and calling study.optimize(...). That is enough to get results, but not enough to debug failures, interpret trial states, reason about pruning, or choose the right storage backend once experiments become more serious.
Understanding the architecture matters because Optuna is not just a function minimizer. It is a coordination layer for optimization logic, experiment state, and execution flow.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.2 A Fast First Win
The quickest way to see the architecture is to run a tiny study and inspect the central objects it produces.

import optuna


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=8)

print("Best params:", study.best_params)
print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))
print("Number of trials:", len(study.trials))
print("First trial state:", study.trials[0].state)

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.3 Architecture at a Glance
An Optuna optimization run consists of several interacting components:
Study
 ├── Trials
 │     ├── Parameters
 │     ├── Objective value
 │     └── Intermediate results
 │
 ├── Sampler
 ├── Pruner
 └── Storage
The Study is the central coordination object. Most of Optuna’s public API is organized around it.





flowchart LR
    A[Study] --> B[Sampler proposes parameters]
    B --> C[Trial object created]
    C --> D[Objective function runs]
    D --> E[Intermediate values reported]
    E --> F[Pruner may stop trial]
    F --> G[Result stored]
    G --> A

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.4 The Study Object
A Study represents one complete optimization experiment.
It manages:

All trials in the experiment.
The sampler.
The pruner.
The storage backend.
The optimization direction.

Creating a study is usually the first Optuna step:

import optuna

study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
print(type(study).__name__)


[I 2026-03-15 07:55:35,586] A new study created in memory with name: no-name-93058f07-f49d-41bc-8650-8a351a8ce226




Study


You can think of the study as the experiment manager. It schedules trials, collects results, tracks the best outcome, and exposes the overall state of the optimization.
Important study operations include:
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)
study.best_params
study.best_value
study.best_trial
study.trials

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.5 The Trial Object
A Trial represents one evaluation of the objective function.
Each trial stores:

Parameter values.
The final objective value.
Intermediate values reported during training or iteration.
Metadata such as state and timestamps.

Conceptually, the flow looks like this:
Trial 1 -> parameters -> objective -> result
Trial 2 -> parameters -> objective -> result
Trial 3 -> parameters -> objective -> result
Trials are created and managed by the study, but the objective function interacts with them directly.

4.5.1 Trial lifecycle
CREATED
  ↓
RUNNING
  ↓
COMPLETE / PRUNED / FAILED
Common states include:



State
Meaning




COMPLETE
Trial finished successfully


PRUNED
Trial was stopped early


FAILED
Trial ended with an error

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.6 The Objective Function
The objective function defines the optimization problem. It receives a Trial object and returns the scalar value to minimize or maximize.

def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2

Inside the objective function, you usually do three things:

Define parameters through trial.suggest_* methods.
Evaluate the model, simulation, or function.
Return the result.

That returned result becomes the stored trial value.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.7 The Search Space and Define-by-Run
The search space is the set of parameters Optuna is allowed to explore.
Optuna uses a define-by-run API, which means the search space is created dynamically during execution rather than declared in a separate static schema.
For example:

def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)
    depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 10)
    optimizer = trial.suggest_categorical("optimizer", ["adam", "sgd"])
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)
    return x**2

This approach is powerful because it supports dynamic and conditional spaces naturally.

def objective(trial):
    model = trial.suggest_categorical("model", ["svm", "rf"])

    if model == "rf":
        depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 10)

    return 0.0

That is exactly the kind of tree-structured space where TPE is especially useful, which is why this chapter connects directly back to The TPE Algorithm.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.8 Samplers
The sampler decides how Optuna chooses new parameter values.
In other words, the sampler implements the optimization strategy.
Examples include:



Sampler
Method




TPESampler
Bayesian-style density estimation


RandomSampler
Random search


CmaEsSampler
Evolutionary strategy


GridSampler
Exhaustive grid search



You can pass a sampler explicitly when creating a study:

study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    sampler=optuna.samplers.TPESampler(),
)

print(type(study.sampler).__name__)


[I 2026-03-15 07:55:35,687] A new study created in memory with name: no-name-eba11095-4da0-4cf0-a2d6-f34c31bd2a66




TPESampler


If you do not specify one, Optuna uses TPE by default for many standard single-objective optimization tasks.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.9 Pruners
Pruners implement early stopping. They decide whether a trial is unlikely to become competitive and should be terminated before finishing.
This is especially useful when objective evaluations are expensive and produce intermediate progress signals, such as training loss by epoch.
Typical use cases include:

Deep learning training.
Boosting methods.
Iterative simulations.

Here is the basic pattern:

def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)

    for step in range(5):
        value = (x - 2) ** 2 + 1 / (step + 1)
        trial.report(value, step)

        if trial.should_prune():
            raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return value

Popular pruners include:



Pruner
Strategy




MedianPruner
Stops trials worse than the historical median


SuccessiveHalvingPruner
Resource-based elimination


HyperbandPruner
Multi-bracket early stopping



We will study pruning in depth later, but architecturally the main point is simple: the pruner depends on intermediate reports from the trial.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.10 Storage Backends
Optuna supports both in-memory and persistent storage.
Storage matters because it enables:

Experiment tracking.
Fault tolerance.
Parallel or distributed optimization.


4.10.1 In-memory storage

study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")


[I 2026-03-15 07:55:35,743] A new study created in memory with name: no-name-6cd1deee-feba-48d9-8b8e-7c6c1509a8e4




This is convenient for quick experiments, but the results disappear when the process ends.


4.10.2 SQLite storage
study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    storage="sqlite:///example.db",
)
This persists the study in a local database file.


4.10.3 Server databases
For larger or distributed workloads, Optuna can use databases such as PostgreSQL or MySQL.
storage = "postgresql://user:pass@host/db"
This allows multiple worker processes or machines to coordinate through shared study storage.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.11 The Internal Optimization Loop
At a high level, Optuna repeatedly runs a loop like this:
while trials_remaining:
    sampler -> propose parameters
    trial -> run objective
    record result
    pruner -> check early stopping
    update sampler
A more detailed view is:
Study starts
   ↓
Sampler suggests parameters
   ↓
Trial object created
   ↓
Objective function executed
   ↓
Intermediate results reported
   ↓
Pruner may stop trial
   ↓
Result stored
   ↓
Sampler updates model
This repeats until a stopping condition is reached, such as a target number of trials, a timeout, or an external stop.






Common Mistake



Do not think of the sampler, trial, and study as separate convenience features. They are parts of one coordinated system. Confusion usually appears when users try to reason about trial behavior without understanding which decisions belong to the study, which belong to the sampler, and which must happen inside the objective function.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.12 A Minimal End-to-End Example
The following example exercises the whole architecture in a compact form:

import optuna


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)
    return (x - 2) ** 2


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=20)

print("Best params:", study.best_params)
print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))
