title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.14 Recap
Optuna’s architecture consists of a small number of key components with distinct responsibilities:



Component
Role




Study
Manages the optimization experiment


Trial
Represents one evaluation of the objective


Objective function
Defines the optimization problem


Search space
Defines the tunable parameters


Sampler
Proposes parameter values


Pruner
Stops weak trials early


Storage
Persists experiment state



The main architectural idea is not complexity, but coordination. Optuna works because these components form an iterative feedback loop between proposing parameters, running evaluations, deciding whether to stop early, and storing results.

title: 4  Optuna Architecture
text:
4.15 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Reach for the Study when you want experiment-level control.
Reach for the Trial when defining parameters or reporting intermediate progress.
Reach for samplers when changing optimization strategy.
Reach for pruners when trial evaluations are iterative and expensive.
Reach for persistent storage when experiments need to survive process boundaries or run in parallel.

Next, Basic Optuna Usage turns this architecture into practical day-to-day Optuna workflows.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.1 Why This Matters
This chapter moves from theory and internal architecture to day-to-day Optuna usage. The goal is straightforward: create a study, define an objective function, declare parameters through the trial.suggest_* API, run optimization, and inspect the results.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to build and run a small Optuna study from scratch and understand how to retrieve the best trial, best parameters, and full trial history.
This chapter assumes you already understand the high-level architecture from Optuna Architecture.
Many optimization libraries look conceptually elegant but remain awkward in practice. Optuna’s main strength is that its core workflow is compact and readable: you write a normal Python function, define parameters where they are used, and let the study coordinate the optimization loop.
That makes it a strong practical tool for hyperparameter tuning, simulation calibration, and general black-box optimization.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.2 A Fast First Win
The quickest useful Optuna program fits on one screen. It creates a study, defines one parameter, runs several trials, and prints the best result.

import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=20)

print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))
print("Best parameters:", study.best_params)

Best value: 0.0003
Best parameters: {'x': 1.9833882692893883}


That is already a complete optimization workflow. Everything else in this chapter is a refinement of that pattern.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.3 Installation and Setup
Optuna is installed with pip:
pip install optuna
To verify the installation:

import optuna
print(optuna.__version__)

4.7.0


Optional packages become useful later:
pip install optuna-dashboard
pip install plotly
Those packages support dashboards and visualization, but they are not required for the core optimization loop.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.4 Creating a Study
The Study object manages the whole optimization process.
The most basic form is:
import optuna

study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
print(type(study).__name__)
The most important initial choice is the optimization direction.



Parameter
Meaning




direction
Either "minimize" or "maximize"



Examples:
study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study = optuna.create_study(direction="maximize")
Use minimization for losses or error metrics. Use maximization for accuracy, reward, or utility-type objectives.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.5 Defining the Objective Function
The objective function defines the problem to be optimized. It receives a Trial object and returns a numeric value.
def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2
Inside the function, three things usually happen:

Parameters are suggested.
A model or mathematical function is evaluated.
A scalar value is returned.

Optuna then tries to minimize or maximize that returned value depending on the study configuration.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.6 Running the Optimization
Once the study and objective function exist, optimization starts with:
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)
The key arguments are:



Parameter
Meaning




objective
The function to optimize


n_trials
The number of objective evaluations



Each trial corresponds to one evaluation of the objective.
Trial 1 -> parameters -> objective value
Trial 2 -> parameters -> objective value
Trial 3 -> parameters -> objective value
...
As trials accumulate, the sampler updates its search strategy using past results.





flowchart LR
    A[Create study] --> B[Define objective]
    B --> C[Run study.optimize]
    C --> D[Sampler suggests parameters]
    D --> E[Objective returns value]
    E --> F[Study records result]
    F --> D

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.7 The Parameter Suggestion API
Optuna defines the search space through trial.suggest_* methods. This is the define-by-run paradigm: parameters are declared inside the objective exactly where they are needed.

5.7.1 suggest_float
Use this for continuous parameters.
x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
This samples a floating-point value between the given bounds.


5.7.2 Log-scaled floats
For parameters spanning several orders of magnitude, use logarithmic sampling.
lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)
This is especially appropriate for quantities such as learning rates, regularization strengths, and tolerances.


5.7.3 suggest_int
Use this for integer-valued parameters.
depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 2, 10)
Typical examples include tree depth, number of layers, and numbers of neighbors.


5.7.4 suggest_categorical
Use this for discrete choices.
optimizer = trial.suggest_categorical(
    "optimizer",
    ["adam", "sgd", "rmsprop"],
)
This is common for model families, optimizers, activations, and preprocessing variants.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.8 A Study with Multiple Parameters
The objective function can define several parameters at once.
def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    y = trial.suggest_float("y", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + (y + 3) ** 2
This turns the search into a two-dimensional optimization problem. In practical machine-learning use, the same pattern extends naturally to many more parameters.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.9 Retrieving Optimization Results
After optimization, the study stores everything needed to inspect the outcome.

5.9.1 Best value
print(study.best_value)
This returns the best objective value found.


5.9.2 Best parameters
print(study.best_params)
Example output might look like:
{'x': 1.98, 'y': -2.97}


5.9.3 Best trial
best = study.best_trial

print(best.params)
print(best.value)
The best trial contains both the winning parameters and the associated metadata.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.10 Inspecting All Trials
The full trial history is available through study.trials.
for trial in study.trials[:5]:
    print(trial.number, round(trial.value, 4), trial.params)
This is useful for:

Debugging.
Visualizing search behavior.
Building experiment summaries.
Understanding whether the sampler is converging toward a promising region.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.11 A Minimal End-to-End Example
The following example puts the full basic workflow in one place:

import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    return (x - 2) ** 2


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=30)

print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))
print("Best parameters:", study.best_params)

Best value: 0.0
Best parameters: {'x': 2.0069441978316847}


This is the smallest workflow that still feels like real Optuna usage.






Common Mistake



Do not define parameters outside the objective function and then expect Optuna to optimize them. If a value does not come from trial.suggest_*, it is not part of the search space and the sampler cannot reason about it.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
section: 5.12 Practical Exercise: Optimize a Mathematical Function
text:
5.12 Practical Exercise: Optimize a Mathematical Function
As a first real exercise, optimize the function
\[
f(x) = (x - 3)^2 + \sin(5x)
\]
using Optuna.

import math
import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)
    return (x - 3) ** 2 + math.sin(5 * x)


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)

print("Best x:", study.best_params)
print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))

Best x: {'x': 3.411030134026742}
Best value: -0.8062


What to observe:

Early trials explore broadly.
Later trials focus more often near strong regions.
The sampler adapts based on what previous trials discovered.

Try extending the exercise in three ways:

Increase the search space from [-5, 5] to [-20, 20] and compare the result.
Replace the one-dimensional objective with a two-parameter function.
Add an integer or categorical parameter to see how naturally Optuna handles mixed spaces.

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.13 Recap
Basic Optuna usage follows a simple five-step pattern:

Install Optuna.
Create a study.
Define the objective function.
Use trial.suggest_* methods to declare parameters.
Run optimization with study.optimize(...).

The most important beginner-facing APIs in this chapter are:



Function
Purpose




suggest_float
Continuous parameters


suggest_int
Integer parameters


suggest_categorical
Discrete choices



The most important result accessors are:

study.best_value
study.best_params
study.best_trial

title: 5  Basic Optuna Usage
text:
5.14 Decision Guide
Use the following rule of thumb:

Reach for a minimal study first when testing a new optimization idea.
Use suggest_float, suggest_int, and suggest_categorical to express the true structure of the search space.
Inspect study.trials whenever the optimizer behaves unexpectedly.
Keep the first objective function simple before scaling up to real pipelines.

Next, Designing Search Spaces focuses on the part of optimization that often matters most in practice: choosing parameter ranges and structures that the optimizer can search effectively.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.1 Why This Matters
This chapter focuses on one of the most important practical skills in optimization: designing a search space that the optimizer can explore effectively. In real Optuna workflows, a well-designed space often matters more than the choice between two strong samplers.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to distinguish continuous, discrete, categorical, conditional, dynamic, and hierarchical search spaces, and choose parameter ranges that reflect both domain knowledge and optimization efficiency.
This chapter builds directly on Basic Optuna Usage and connects back to why The TPE Algorithm works well with tree-structured spaces.
Suppose you are tuning a model and choose a learning-rate range from 1e-8 to 10, a tree depth from 1 to 1000, and several conditional parameters that are never active together. Even a strong optimizer will waste trials in irrelevant or pathological regions.
Search-space design matters because the optimizer can only search the space you give it. If the space is poorly structured, the optimization loop becomes inefficient no matter how sophisticated the sampler is.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.2 A Fast First Win
The easiest way to see search-space design in action is to compare a few parameter types inside one small Optuna objective.

import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
    depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 2, 8)
    optimizer = trial.suggest_categorical("optimizer", ["adam", "sgd"])
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)

    penalty = 0.0 if optimizer == "adam" else 0.2
    return (x - 2) ** 2 + 0.01 * depth + penalty + abs(lr - 1e-2)


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=20)

print("Best params:", study.best_params)
print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))

Best params: {'x': 2.0646547029516213, 'depth': 7, 'optimizer': 'adam', 'lr': 0.0005932423243772284}
Best value: 0.0836


This example already shows the main idea of the chapter: the search space is not just a range of numbers. It is a structured description of what the optimizer is allowed to try.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.3 Continuous, Discrete, and Categorical Parameters
Search spaces usually combine different parameter types.

6.3.1 Continuous parameters
Continuous parameters represent real-valued ranges.
\[
x \in [-10, 10]
\]
In Optuna:
x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10, 10)
Typical continuous parameters include:



Parameter
Example range




Learning rate
0.0001 to 0.1


Regularization
1e-6 to 1


Dropout
0 to 0.5





6.3.2 Discrete parameters
Discrete parameters often take integer values.
\[
depth \in \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10\}
\]
In Optuna:
depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 1, 10)
Examples include tree depth, numbers of layers, and neighborhood sizes.


6.3.3 Categorical parameters
Categorical parameters represent choices among named options.
model = trial.suggest_categorical(
    "model",
    ["xgboost", "random_forest", "svm"],
)
These are common for model families, optimizers, activations, kernels, and preprocessing decisions.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.4 Why Log-Scaled Parameters Matter
Some parameters span multiple orders of magnitude. Learning rates are the canonical example.
Instead of sampling uniformly over
\[
[10^{-5}, 10^{-1}]
\]
you usually want to sample in log-space:
lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)
This avoids oversampling the large end of the range and better reflects how such parameters behave in practice.
Typical log-scaled parameters include:



Parameter
Example range




Learning rate
1e-5 to 1e-1


Regularization
1e-6 to 10


Kernel width
1e-3 to 10

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.5 Conditional Parameters
Some parameters only make sense when another choice is active.
For example, if the selected model is a random forest, you may want to optimize n_trees and depth. If the selected model is an SVM, those parameters are irrelevant, and instead you care about C and kernel.
Optuna handles this naturally with define-by-run logic:

def objective(trial):
    model = trial.suggest_categorical("model", ["rf", "svm"])

    if model == "rf":
        n_trees = trial.suggest_int("n_trees", 50, 200)
        depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 10)
        return 0.001 * n_trees + 0.1 * depth

    c_value = trial.suggest_float("C", 1e-3, 10, log=True)
    kernel = trial.suggest_categorical("kernel", ["linear", "rbf"])
    kernel_penalty = 0.0 if kernel == "rbf" else 0.2
    return abs(c_value - 1.0) + kernel_penalty

This creates a conditional branch inside the search space. Structurally, that is exactly the kind of tree-shaped configuration TPE is designed to handle well.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.6 Dynamic Search Spaces
Optuna also allows the number of active parameters to depend on earlier choices.
For example:

def objective(trial):
    n_layers = trial.suggest_int("n_layers", 1, 4)

    total_units = 0
    for i in range(n_layers):
        units = trial.suggest_int(f"units_layer_{i}", 32, 256)
        total_units += units

    return abs(total_units - 320)

Here, the dimensionality of the space changes from trial to trial because the number of active layer parameters depends on n_layers.
This flexibility is useful for neural architecture search, pipeline configuration, and other structured optimization problems.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.7 Hierarchical Search Spaces
Hierarchical spaces are multi-level search spaces where one decision activates a whole family of sub-decisions.

def objective(trial):
    model = trial.suggest_categorical("model", ["xgb", "rf", "svm"])

    if model == "xgb":
        max_depth = trial.suggest_int("max_depth", 3, 10)
        eta = trial.suggest_float("eta", 1e-3, 0.3, log=True)
        return 0.1 * max_depth + abs(eta - 0.05)

    if model == "rf":
        n_estimators = trial.suggest_int("n_estimators", 50, 300)
        return abs(n_estimators - 180)

    c_value = trial.suggest_float("C", 1e-3, 10, log=True)
    return abs(c_value - 1.5)

This kind of hierarchy is common in AutoML workflows and whole-pipeline optimization.





flowchart TD
    A[Choose model] --> B[XGBoost branch]
    A --> C[Random Forest branch]
    A --> D[SVM branch]
    B --> E[max_depth, eta]
    C --> F[n_estimators, depth]
    D --> G[C, kernel]

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.8 Example: Neural Architecture Search
Optuna is often used for neural architecture search precisely because define-by-run spaces can express variable structure compactly.
def objective(trial):
    n_layers = trial.suggest_int("n_layers", 1, 4)

    layers = []
    for i in range(n_layers):
        units = trial.suggest_int(f"units_{i}", 32, 256)
        dropout = trial.suggest_float(f"dropout_{i}", 0.0, 0.5)
        layers.append((units, dropout))

    return len(layers)
Each trial can represent a different architecture, which would be cumbersome to describe in a rigid static search-space definition.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.9 Example: Algorithm Configuration
Search spaces do not have to be about model hyperparameters only. They can also represent algorithmic design choices.
solver = trial.suggest_categorical("solver", ["gradient", "newton"])

if solver == "gradient":
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)
else:
    damping = trial.suggest_float("damping", 1e-3, 1)
This lets Optuna search not only over numeric hyperparameters, but over whole families of algorithm behavior.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
section: 6.10 Best Practices for Search Space Design
text:
6.10 Best Practices for Search Space Design

6.10.1 Use log scales for multiplicative parameters
Bad:
lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 0.00001, 0.1)
Better:
lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)


6.10.2 Avoid absurdly large ranges
Bad:
depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 1, 1000)
Better:
depth = trial.suggest_int("depth", 3, 15)
Large unrealistic ranges waste trials and slow convergence.


6.10.3 Use domain knowledge aggressively
Good search spaces reflect:

Theoretical constraints.
Empirical experience.
Practical operating limits.







Common Mistake



Do not treat the search space as a neutral container. It encodes assumptions about what values are plausible. A badly chosen space can make a strong sampler look weak, hide the true optimum behind useless volume, or spend most trials on configurations you would never deploy.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.11 Practical Exercise: Conditional Hyperparameter Optimization
As a hands-on exercise, optimize a tiny conditional problem where the study chooses between two model families.

import math
import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    model = trial.suggest_categorical("model", ["quadratic", "sin"])
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)

    if model == "quadratic":
        return (x - 2) ** 2

    return math.sin(x) + 1


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=60)

print("Best parameters:", study.best_params)
print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))

Best parameters: {'model': 'sin', 'x': -1.5687253960769634}
Best value: 0.0


This example demonstrates:

Categorical model selection.
Conditional objective logic.
Flexible search-space definition inside the objective.

Try extending it by adding model-specific parameters that only apply to one branch.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.12 Recap
Effective search spaces often include a mix of:



Feature
Why it matters




Continuous parameters
Capture real-valued behavior


Discrete parameters
Respect integer constraints


Log distributions
Handle multi-scale parameters


Conditional parameters
Support structured model choices


Dynamic spaces
Allow variable architecture or pipeline depth


Hierarchical spaces
Enable multi-level AutoML workflows



The practical lesson is simple: a good search space can improve optimization more than switching between two strong optimizers.

title: 6  Designing Search Spaces
text:
6.13 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Use continuous ranges for genuinely real-valued quantities.
Use integer or categorical parameters when the problem structure demands them.
Use log=True whenever scale matters more than absolute distance.
Use conditional and hierarchical branches when different model families require different parameter sets.
Shrink overly broad ranges whenever domain knowledge allows it.

Next, Samplers focuses on the optimization engines that search these spaces and how their behavior differs in practice.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.1 Why This Matters
This chapter explains the optimization engines that actually choose parameter values in Optuna. Once the objective function and search space are defined, the sampler determines how the study explores the space, what it learns from previous trials, and how quickly it tends to converge toward strong regions.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to describe the main Optuna sampler families, understand when to use each one, and explain how their exploration behavior changes in high-dimensional or structured search spaces.
This chapter builds directly on Designing Search Spaces, because sampler performance only makes sense in the context of the space being searched.
Once your search space is well designed, the next practical question is which optimization engine should drive the search. The answer depends on the structure of the problem: mixed or categorical spaces, continuous-only optimization, exhaustive benchmarking, or multiple objectives all push toward different samplers.
Optuna exposes those choices through a unified interface, which makes it easy to compare strategies without rewriting the rest of the optimization workflow.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.2 A Fast First Win
The quickest way to see what a sampler changes is to run the same objective with two different search strategies.

import math
import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)
    return (x - 3) ** 2 + math.sin(5 * x)


study_tpe = optuna.create_study(
    sampler=optuna.samplers.TPESampler(),
    direction="minimize",
)
study_tpe.optimize(objective, n_trials=40)

study_random = optuna.create_study(
    sampler=optuna.samplers.RandomSampler(),
    direction="minimize",
)
study_random.optimize(objective, n_trials=40)

print("TPE best:", round(study_tpe.best_value, 4))
print("Random best:", round(study_random.best_value, 4))

TPE best: -0.8064
Random best: -0.2021


This does not prove that one sampler is always better. It shows the central point of the chapter: the sampler is the optimization policy that drives trial selection.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.3 What a Sampler Does
In Optuna, the sampler is the component responsible for proposing new parameter values.
Conceptually, the loop looks like this:
Sampler -> proposes parameters
Trial -> evaluates objective
Sampler -> updates internal strategy
At a higher level, each trial follows the same pattern:
1. sampler proposes parameters
2. objective function evaluates them
3. result is stored
4. sampler learns from results
The crucial difference between samplers is how they balance exploration and exploitation.





flowchart LR
    A[Sampler] --> B[Parameter proposal]
    B --> C[Trial runs objective]
    C --> D[Result stored]
    D --> E[Sampler updates strategy]
    E --> A

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.4 TPE Sampler
Optuna’s default optimizer is the Tree-structured Parzen Estimator, or TPE.
TPE is a Bayesian-style optimization method based on density estimation. Instead of modeling
\[
p(y \mid x)
\]
it models
\[
p(x \mid y)
\]
and uses the separation between good and bad trials to rank new candidates.
The core idea is:
good trials -> build density l(x)
bad trials -> build density g(x)
maximize l(x) / g(x)
Characteristics:



Property
Behavior




Type
Bayesian-style density estimation


Exploration
Adaptive


Scalability
Good


Categorical support
Excellent


High-dimensional robustness
Good



Use TPE for:

General machine-learning hyperparameter tuning.
Mixed parameter spaces.
Categorical variables.
Conditional or tree-structured spaces.

In practice, TPE is the default because it is the most versatile general-purpose sampler in Optuna.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.5 Random Sampler
RandomSampler performs pure random search.
sampler = optuna.samplers.RandomSampler()

study = optuna.create_study(
    sampler=sampler,
    direction="minimize",
)
It does not learn from previous trials. Each proposal is independent.
Characteristics:



Property
Behavior




Type
Random search


Exploration
Maximum


Exploitation
None


Complexity
Minimal



Use random search for:

Baseline comparisons.
Early pipeline debugging.
Very small or poorly understood spaces.
Cases where you want a neutral reference strategy.

Random search is often stronger than people expect, especially as a baseline.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.6 CMA-ES Sampler
CMA-ES stands for Covariance Matrix Adaptation Evolution Strategy.
It is an evolutionary method that maintains a multivariate Gaussian search distribution and updates that distribution based on successful samples.
Conceptually:
sample population
evaluate objective
select strong candidates
update mean and covariance
repeat
Characteristics:



Property
Behavior




Type
Evolutionary strategy


Exploration
Adaptive


Handles correlations
Very well


Continuous spaces
Excellent



Use CMA-ES for:

Continuous optimization.
Medium-dimensional spaces.
Expensive smooth objectives.
Problems where parameter correlations matter.

Limitations:

It is not a natural fit for categorical variables.
It often has a slower warm-up phase than simpler approaches.

Illustrative setup:
sampler = optuna.samplers.CmaEsSampler()
study = optuna.create_study(sampler=sampler, direction="minimize")

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.7 Grid Sampler
GridSampler performs exhaustive search over a predefined grid.
search_space = {
    "x": [-2, 0, 2],
    "y": [-1, 1],
}

sampler = optuna.samplers.GridSampler(search_space)
It does not learn from past results. Its purpose is deterministic coverage of a fixed set of combinations.
Characteristics:



Property
Behavior




Type
Exhaustive search


Exploration
Deterministic


Learning
None



Use grid search when:

The space is genuinely small.
Reproducible coverage matters more than efficiency.
You want a controlled benchmark.

Its weakness is obvious: it becomes impractical very quickly as the number of parameters grows.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.8 NSGA-II for Multi-Objective Optimization
NSGAIISampler is used for multi-objective optimization.
Instead of optimizing a single objective
\[
f(x)
\]
you optimize multiple objectives simultaneously, such as:
\[
f_1(x), f_2(x)
\]
For example, you may want to maximize accuracy while minimizing model size.
The goal is not a single best point, but a Pareto frontier: a set of trade-off solutions where improving one objective would worsen another.
study = optuna.create_study(
    directions=["minimize", "maximize"],
    sampler=optuna.samplers.NSGAIISampler(),
)
Characteristics:



Property
Behavior




Type
Evolutionary multi-objective optimization


Output
Pareto set


Exploration
Population-based

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.9 Exploration Behavior Across Samplers
Different samplers explore the space differently.



Sampler
Exploration strategy




Random
Uniform sampling


TPE
Probabilistic density modeling


CMA-ES
Adaptive Gaussian search


Grid
Deterministic enumeration


NSGA-II
Evolutionary population search



These choices influence:

Convergence speed.
Sample efficiency.
Robustness to local structure.
Suitability for mixed or high-dimensional spaces.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.10 Samplers in High-Dimensional Spaces
High-dimensional optimization is difficult because search volume grows rapidly with the number of parameters.
If each parameter has \(k\) relevant configurations and the dimension is \(d\), then the effective search burden grows roughly like:
\[
k^d
\]
That is why high-dimensional behavior matters so much when choosing a sampler.
In broad practical terms:



Sampler
High-dimensional behavior




Random
Moderate baseline


TPE
Often strong in mixed spaces


CMA-ES
Useful in continuous spaces, but not universal


Grid
Usually poor



TPE often performs well in mixed, structured problems with dozens of parameters, which is one reason it became Optuna’s default choice.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.11 Choosing the Right Sampler
As a practical rule of thumb:



Situation
Recommended sampler




General hyperparameter tuning
TPE


Baseline comparison
Random


Continuous optimization
CMA-ES


Small discrete space
Grid


Multi-objective problem
NSGA-II



The default recommendation remains simple:

Start with TPE unless you have a concrete reason to choose something else.







Common Mistake



Do not compare samplers on a poorly designed or unfairly scaled search space and then conclude that one algorithm is intrinsically better. Sampler performance depends heavily on search-space structure, budget, parameter types, and objective noise.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.12 Practical Exercise: Compare Samplers
Compare TPE and random search on the same one-dimensional objective:
\[
f(x) = (x - 3)^2 + \sin(5x)
\]

import math
import optuna

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5, 5)
    return (x - 3) ** 2 + math.sin(5 * x)


study_tpe = optuna.create_study(
    sampler=optuna.samplers.TPESampler(),
    direction="minimize",
)
study_tpe.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)

study_random = optuna.create_study(
    sampler=optuna.samplers.RandomSampler(),
    direction="minimize",
)
study_random.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)

print("TPE best:", round(study_tpe.best_value, 4))
print("Random best:", round(study_random.best_value, 4))

TPE best: -0.8077
Random best: -0.7965


What to observe:

Early trials often look similar.
TPE usually begins exploiting strong regions sooner.
Random search continues broad exploration throughout the run.

If you want to deepen the exercise, repeat the comparison with a larger trial budget or a two-parameter function.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.13 Recap
Samplers determine how Optuna chooses new parameter values.



Sampler
Best fit




TPE
General hyperparameter tuning


Random
Baselines and debugging


CMA-ES
Continuous optimization


Grid
Small discrete spaces


NSGA-II
Multi-objective optimization



TPE is the default because it balances exploration and exploitation, handles categorical and conditional spaces well, and remains practical in many high-dimensional problems.

title: 7  Samplers
text:
7.14 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Use TPE as the general default.
Use random search to establish a baseline or sanity check a pipeline.
Use CMA-ES when the space is mostly continuous and parameter correlations matter.
Use grid only when the space is intentionally tiny.
Use NSGA-II when there is no single objective to optimize.

Next, Pruning turns to one of Optuna’s most important efficiency features: stopping weak trials early to save compute.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.1 Why This Matters
This chapter explains how Optuna avoids wasting computation on trials that are clearly underperforming. In many optimization problems, especially machine-learning workflows, each trial unfolds over a sequence of steps such as epochs, boosting rounds, or simulation iterations. Pruning allows Optuna to stop weak trials early and redirect resources to stronger candidates.
By the end of the chapter, you should be able to explain how intermediate results are reported, how pruning decisions are made, and when to use median pruning, successive halving, Hyperband, and related strategies.
This chapter builds directly on Samplers. Samplers decide what to try next; pruners decide whether the current trial is still worth continuing.
Suppose two neural-network configurations are both scheduled to train for 100 epochs. One shows promising validation loss from the first few epochs. The other is clearly lagging. If you still force both to train to completion, most of your optimization budget gets spent confirming something you already suspected.
Pruning matters because early stopping at the trial level is often the biggest practical speedup in hyperparameter optimization.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.2 A Fast First Win
The simplest pruning pattern is small enough to fit in one function: report an intermediate metric, ask the pruner whether the trial should continue, and stop early if not.

import optuna
import random

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-4, 1e-1, log=True)
    loss = 1.2 + 0.2 * lr

    for epoch in range(20):
        loss *= random.uniform(0.82, 1.02)
        trial.report(loss, epoch)

        if trial.should_prune():
            raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return loss


study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    pruner=optuna.pruners.MedianPruner(),
)
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=30)

completed = sum(t.state == optuna.trial.TrialState.COMPLETE for t in study.trials)
pruned = sum(t.state == optuna.trial.TrialState.PRUNED for t in study.trials)

print("Completed trials:", completed)
print("Pruned trials:", pruned)

Completed trials: 8
Pruned trials: 22


This captures the full pruning pattern used in real training loops. The only difference in larger systems is that the intermediate metric usually comes from a real model evaluation.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.3 Why Pruning Helps
Consider two training runs after only a few epochs:



Epoch
Trial A loss
Trial B loss




1
0.92
1.40


2
0.91
1.35


3
0.90
1.31



Trial B is already much weaker. If both trials still train to epoch 100, the optimizer wastes time, compute, and often expensive accelerator resources.
Pruning makes the optimization budget more selective:

Weak trials terminate early.
Promising trials receive more compute.
The study covers more configurations with the same budget.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.4 Intermediate Results
Pruning only works when the objective provides progress updates during execution. Optuna uses:
trial.report(value, step)
where:

value is the current metric, such as validation loss or accuracy.
step is the iteration number, such as epoch or boosting round.

Example:
trial.report(validation_loss, epoch)
Without intermediate reports, the pruner has nothing to evaluate.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.5 The Pruning Decision
After reporting an intermediate value, the objective asks Optuna whether the trial should continue:
trial.should_prune()
If the answer is yes, the standard Optuna pattern is:
raise optuna.TrialPruned()
The full loop looks like this:
for epoch in range(100):
    loss = train_one_epoch()
    trial.report(loss, epoch)

    if trial.should_prune():
        raise optuna.TrialPruned()
That exception is not an error condition in the usual sense. It is the normal control-flow signal for a trial that has been intentionally stopped.





flowchart LR
    A[Training step] --> B[Compute intermediate metric]
    B --> C["trial.report(value, step)"]
    C --> D{"trial.should_prune()?"}
    D -->|Yes| E[Raise TrialPruned]
    D -->|No| F[Continue training]
    F --> A

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.6 Median Pruning
MedianPruner is the simplest widely useful pruning strategy.
It compares the current trial against the median performance of earlier completed trials at the same step. If the current trial is worse than that reference, it is pruned.
Example at epoch 5:



Trial
Loss




1
0.70


2
0.65


3
0.75



The median is roughly:
\[
0.70
\]
If a new trial reports a loss like:
\[
0.90
\]
it is a natural candidate for pruning.
Advantages of median pruning:

Simple.
Robust.
Often effective with little tuning.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.7 Successive Halving
Successive halving is a resource-allocation strategy rather than a purely statistical threshold rule.
The idea is:

Start many trials with small budgets.
Evaluate them quickly.
Keep only the best fraction.
Allocate larger budgets to the survivors.

Example schedule:



Stage
Trials
Epochs




1
100
5


2
25
20


3
6
100



Most trials die early. Only promising trials receive full resources.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.8 Hyperband
Hyperband improves on successive halving by running multiple resource-allocation schedules with different trade-offs.
Some brackets favor many short trials. Others favor fewer but longer trials. This makes Hyperband more robust when you do not know the right allocation schedule in advance.
Conceptually:
multiple successive-halving brackets
each bracket explores a different resource schedule
best trials survive longer
Hyperband is one of the most widely used pruning strategies because it balances aggressiveness and robustness across a broad range of workloads.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.9 Asynchronous Pruning
In distributed optimization, trials may run in parallel on different workers.
If pruning required global synchronization, idle time would accumulate quickly. Optuna therefore supports asynchronous pruning behavior, where trials are judged independently as their intermediate results arrive.
This helps with:

Better resource utilization.
Reduced waiting time.
More efficient parallel studies.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.10 Pruners Available in Optuna
Optuna includes several pruner classes.



Pruner
Strategy




MedianPruner
Median-based pruning


SuccessiveHalvingPruner
Multi-stage elimination


HyperbandPruner
Multiple halving schedules


ThresholdPruner
Absolute thresholds


NopPruner
Disables pruning



A basic setup looks like this:
pruner = optuna.pruners.MedianPruner()

study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    pruner=pruner,
)

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.11 Integration with Training Loops
Pruning is especially valuable for iterative algorithms such as neural-network training, boosting, and simulation-based learning loops.
The integration pattern is always the same:
for epoch in range(50):
    train(model)
    val_loss = evaluate(model)

    trial.report(val_loss, epoch)

    if trial.should_prune():
        raise optuna.TrialPruned()
Three lines matter architecturally:

trial.report(...) sends the progress signal.
trial.should_prune() asks for a pruning decision.
TrialPruned stops the current trial early.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.12 Example: Deep-Learning-Style Pruning Loop
Here is a simplified training-style objective with pruning:
def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-2, log=True)

    model = build_model()
    optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=lr)

    for epoch in range(20):
        train_epoch(model)
        val_loss = validate(model)

        trial.report(val_loss, epoch)

        if trial.should_prune():
            raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return val_loss
This is intentionally shown as an illustrative code fence rather than an executable cell. The important point is the structural integration, not the specific framework.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.13 Practical Exercise: Simulate Pruning
Use the following runnable example to see pruning behavior in a small study:

import optuna
import random

optuna.logging.set_verbosity(optuna.logging.WARNING)


def objective(trial):
    lr = trial.suggest_float("lr", 1e-5, 1e-1, log=True)
    loss = 1.0 + 0.05 * lr

    for epoch in range(20):
        loss *= random.uniform(0.8, 1.1)
        trial.report(loss, epoch)

        if trial.should_prune():
            raise optuna.TrialPruned()

    return loss


study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    pruner=optuna.pruners.MedianPruner(),
)
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=50)

completed = sum(t.state == optuna.trial.TrialState.COMPLETE for t in study.trials)
pruned = sum(t.state == optuna.trial.TrialState.PRUNED for t in study.trials)

print("Completed:", completed)
print("Pruned:", pruned)

Completed: 7
Pruned: 43


What to observe:

Many trials stop early.
Only a subset finishes.
The study spends more effort on promising regions.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.14 Recap
Pruning is one of Optuna’s most important efficiency features. It reduces wasted computation by stopping weak trials before they consume their full budget.
The key pieces are:



Function or class
Purpose




trial.report()
Send intermediate results


trial.should_prune()
Ask whether to stop early


optuna.TrialPruned
Terminate the trial cleanly



Common pruning strategies include:



Pruner
Best fit




Median
Simple and robust


Successive Halving
Budget allocation


Hyperband
Multi-schedule early stopping



Pruning is especially useful in deep learning, boosting, and any iterative workload where weak trials reveal themselves long before completion.

title: 8  Pruning
text:
8.15 Decision Guide
Use the following mental model:

Use a pruner whenever the objective produces meaningful intermediate metrics.
Start with MedianPruner for a simple default.
Consider successive halving or Hyperband when budget allocation is central to the problem.
Skip pruning when the objective only returns a final value and has no useful intermediate signal.

Next, Advanced Optimization Techniques extends the framework to more complex workflows such as multi-objective search, constrained optimization, and custom optimization components.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
text:
9.1 Why This Matters
This chapter shows how Optuna moves beyond single-objective hyperparameter tuning into richer optimization workflows. By the end, you should be able to model dynamic search spaces, reason about Pareto-optimal trade-offs, and decide when to use constrained optimization or custom optimization components.
In production systems, the best configuration is rarely the one with the highest validation score alone. You may need to balance accuracy against latency, memory footprint, energy usage, fairness constraints, or engineering limits imposed by deployment.
That is where Optuna’s more advanced features matter. They let you optimize over search spaces whose structure depends on runtime decisions, retain multiple equally valid trade-off solutions, and inject domain-specific search or pruning logic when the built-in algorithms are not enough.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.2 Fast First Win: A Multi-Objective Study

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.3 When Does the Search Space Become Part of the Algorithm?

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
text:
9.4 What Changes in Multi-Objective Optimization?
In single-objective optimization, we solve:
\[
x^* = \arg\min_{x \in \mathcal{X}} f(x)
\]
In multi-objective optimization, the target becomes a vector:
\[
\mathbf{f}(x) = \left(f_1(x), f_2(x), \ldots, f_k(x)\right)
\]
There is usually no total ordering over all candidates, because improving one objective often worsens another. Instead, we use dominance.
A candidate \(x_a\) dominates \(x_b\) if it is no worse on every objective and strictly better on at least one.
The Pareto front is the set of non-dominated solutions. In Optuna, those solutions are exposed through study.best_trials for multi-objective studies.



Configuration
Accuracy
Latency
Dominated?




A
0.95
45 ms
No


B
0.94
18 ms
No


C
0.92
16 ms
No


D
0.93
40 ms
Yes



Configuration D is dominated because another configuration can be both more accurate and faster.





flowchart LR
    A[Sample candidate] --> B[Evaluate all objectives]
    B --> C{Dominated by current frontier?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Keep for history only]
    C -->|No| E[Add to Pareto front]
    E --> F[Remove newly dominated points]
    D --> G[Update sampler state]
    F --> G
    G --> A






For advanced practitioners, this matters because model selection stops being a single scalar decision. The optimizer produces a set of candidates, and downstream decision-making becomes a policy question: which trade-off is operationally acceptable?

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.5 How Should You Read a Pareto Front?
text:
9.5 How Should You Read a Pareto Front?
A Pareto front is useful only if you treat it as a decision surface rather than as a ranking.
Three practical interpretations are common:

Knee-point selection: choose the point where small objective gains start costing a lot elsewhere.
Budget-based selection: choose the best solution under a latency, memory, or cost cap.
Portfolio selection: keep several frontier points for A/B tests or environment-specific deployment.

That distinction matters because the optimizer cannot decide your business trade-off for you. It can only expose the feasible frontier cleanly.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.6 How Do Constraints Enter the Search?
text:
9.6 How Do Constraints Enter the Search?
Some requirements are not preferences but hard limits. You may need:

latency under 20 ms
model size under 200 MB
memory usage below device capacity
parameter combinations that satisfy physical or safety rules

There are two broad strategies.

9.6.1 Penalty-Based Constraints
The simplest approach is to encode constraint violations directly in the objective value.

def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5.0, 5.0)
    y = trial.suggest_float("y", -5.0, 5.0)

    value = x**2 + y**2
    violation = max(0.0, x + y - 2.0)

    return value + 1000.0 * violation

This is easy to implement, but it mixes feasibility and quality into one scalar. A large penalty may make the landscape unstable, while a small penalty may let invalid solutions survive too long.


9.6.2 Constraint-Aware Multi-Objective Search
For algorithms that support explicit constraints, it is cleaner to separate objective values from feasibility information.

def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -5.0, 5.0)
    y = trial.suggest_float("y", -5.0, 5.0)
    trial.set_user_attr("constraint", x + y - 2.0)
    return x**2, y**2


def constraints_func(frozen_trial):
    return (frozen_trial.user_attrs["constraint"],)


sampler = optuna.samplers.NSGAIISampler(constraints_func=constraints_func)

/tmp/ipykernel_117165/2264972085.py:12: ExperimentalWarning: Argument ``constraints_func`` is an experimental feature. The interface can change in the future.
  sampler = optuna.samplers.NSGAIISampler(constraints_func=constraints_func)


The rule is simple: negative or zero values indicate feasible solutions, while positive values indicate violations. This keeps the optimization objective readable and the constraint handling explicit.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.7 When Should You Write a Custom Sampler?

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.8 When Should You Write a Custom Pruner?
text:
9.8 When Should You Write a Custom Pruner?
The same caution applies to pruners. Optuna already ships with median pruning, successive halving, Hyperband, and related policies discussed in Pruning. A custom pruner is worthwhile only when your stopping logic depends on domain knowledge unavailable to the built-in rules.
Examples include:

simulation pipelines with custom convergence criteria
training systems with resource-aware stop rules
domain-specific safety checks that should terminate invalid trials early

from optuna.pruners import BasePruner


class ThresholdPruner(BasePruner):
    def prune(self, study, trial):
        if trial.last_step is None:
            return False

        latest_value = trial.intermediate_values[trial.last_step]
        return latest_value > 0.5
The engineering question is not whether you can subclass BasePruner, but whether the custom rule produces better resource allocation than the standard pruners with less maintenance risk.






Common Mistake



Do not treat multi-objective optimization as if it should produce one universally best trial. If you immediately collapse several objectives into a single score without a clear reason, you throw away the main benefit of the Pareto formulation and hide the trade-offs you were supposed to inspect.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
section: 9.9 Practical Exercise: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization with NSGA-II
text:
9.9 Practical Exercise: Constrained Multi-Objective Optimization with NSGA-II
Extend the fast first win into a more realistic workflow.
Build a study with two objectives and one explicit feasibility rule:
\[
g(x, y) = x + y - 1 \le 0
\]
Use NSGA-II to minimize:
\[
f_1(x, y) = (x - 1)^2 + y^2
\]
and
\[
f_2(x, y) = x^2 + (y - 1)^2
\]
Work through the exercise in four passes:

Reproduce the study with directions=["minimize", "minimize"].
Attach the constraint value to each trial with trial.set_user_attr(...) and pass constraints_func into NSGAIISampler.
Print the feasible Pareto-optimal trials and verify that infeasible points are filtered out of the frontier.
Change the constraint to \(x + y - 0.25 \le 0\) and compare how the frontier shrinks.

Questions to answer after the run:

Which points survive after the feasible region tightens?
Does the new frontier move toward one axis or remain balanced?
If you were deploying under a strict latency or memory budget, which frontier point would you pick and why?

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
text:
9.10 Recap
This chapter introduced the Optuna features that matter once optimization becomes structurally complex.

Dynamic parameter spaces let the search space depend on earlier trial decisions.
Multi-objective studies return Pareto-optimal sets rather than one scalar optimum.
Constraint handling separates feasibility from pure objective quality.
Custom samplers and pruners are extension points, but they shift algorithmic responsibility onto you.

title: 9  Advanced Optimization Techniques
text:
9.11 Decision Guide
Use the simplest mechanism that matches the problem.

Use dynamic spaces when the model family or pipeline structure depends on trial choices.
Use multi-objective optimization when competing metrics should remain visible instead of being collapsed prematurely.
Use penalty methods for quick prototypes, but prefer explicit constraint handling when feasibility is a first-class concern.
Use custom samplers or pruners only when built-in Optuna components cannot express the domain logic you need.

The next chapter turns these advanced runs into something inspectable. In Optuna Visualization and Diagnostics, we will analyze optimization histories, parameter importance, and failure patterns so that search behavior becomes easier to interpret and improve.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
text:
10.1 Why This Matters
This chapter is about reading an optimization process rather than merely running one. By the end, you should be able to use Optuna’s visualization tools to diagnose convergence, identify influential parameters, detect bad search-space design, and decide when a study needs redesign rather than more trials.
An optimizer can fail quietly. A study may appear to run correctly while wasting budget on irrelevant parameters, spending trials in the wrong scale, or bouncing around a noisy objective with little real progress.
That is why diagnostics matter. Once you can inspect optimization history, parameter importance, and interactions, you stop treating hyperparameter tuning as a black box and start treating it as an iterative engineering process.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.2 Fast First Win: Build a Study Worth Diagnosing

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
text:
10.3 Which Tools Does Optuna Provide?
Optuna exposes its plotting tools through optuna.visualization, which generates interactive Plotly figures.
import optuna.visualization as vis

vis.plot_optimization_history(study)
vis.plot_param_importances(study)
vis.plot_slice(study)
vis.plot_parallel_coordinate(study)
vis.plot_contour(study)
If Plotly is not already installed in your environment, add it explicitly:
pip install plotly
For advanced studies, these plots are not presentation artifacts. They are debugging tools for your search strategy.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.4 How Should You Read Optimization History?
text:
10.4 How Should You Read Optimization History?
The optimization history plot shows objective value as a function of trial number.
optuna.visualization.plot_optimization_history(study)
Two different curves matter conceptually:

Trial values: the raw objective values observed at each evaluation.
Best-so-far values: the incumbent trajectory, which shows whether the optimizer is still making meaningful progress.






flowchart LR
    A[Run study] --> B[Plot optimization history]
    B --> C{Improvement still happening?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Keep budget or refine sampler]
    C -->|No| E[Inspect space and diagnostics]
    E --> F[Reduce range or redesign parameters]
    D --> G[Continue optimization]
    F --> G






Typical interpretations:

Healthy convergence: the best-so-far curve keeps improving, even if raw trial values remain noisy.
Stagnation: many trials pass without a better incumbent, suggesting a poor search space, insufficient exploration, or an almost-exhausted budget.
High noise: raw values swing widely, but the incumbent barely changes, indicating evaluation instability or stochastic objectives.

Convergence plots are especially useful when read alongside Pruning, because apparent stagnation may mean the search space is bad, not merely that pruning is aggressive.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.5 What Does Parameter Importance Actually Mean?
text:
10.5 What Does Parameter Importance Actually Mean?
Parameter importance estimates how much each parameter contributes to variation in the objective.
optuna.visualization.plot_param_importances(study)
Optuna commonly uses fANOVA-style reasoning. At a high level, if total objective variation is \(\mathrm{Var}(f(x))\), the importance of parameter \(x_i\) is approximated by the fraction of variance explained by changes in that parameter:
\[
\mathrm{Importance}(x_i) = \frac{\mathrm{Var}_{x_i}\left(\mathbb{E}[f(x) \mid x_i]\right)}{\mathrm{Var}(f(x))}
\]
Interpretation is practical rather than philosophical:

High importance suggests the parameter meaningfully changes outcomes in the explored region.
Low importance suggests the parameter is either genuinely weak or not yet explored in the right regime.

This distinction matters. Low importance does not automatically mean the parameter is useless. It may only mean that the current search space or trial budget has not revealed its effect yet.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.6 How Do Slice Plots Expose Useful Structure?
text:
10.6 How Do Slice Plots Expose Useful Structure?
Slice plots visualize one parameter against the objective.
optuna.visualization.plot_slice(study)
Use them to answer questions such as:

Is there a narrow region where the objective improves sharply?
Is the relationship monotonic, U-shaped, or mostly flat?
Are strong results concentrated near one boundary of the search range?

This is one of the quickest ways to catch poorly designed spaces from Designing Search Spaces. If all strong trials cluster near one edge, your range is probably too wide or mis-scaled.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.7 When Do Parallel Coordinates Help More Than Slices?
text:
10.7 When Do Parallel Coordinates Help More Than Slices?
Slice plots show one parameter at a time. Parallel coordinate plots help when performance depends on combinations of parameters.
optuna.visualization.plot_parallel_coordinate(study)
Each line represents a single trial crossing multiple parameter axes and the objective value. This is useful for discovering patterns such as:

strong trials combining high learning rates with low regularization
a parameter becoming useful only when another parameter is within a narrow interval
certain regions consistently producing weak results regardless of other choices

For advanced practitioners, this is the bridge between raw visualization and search-space redesign: you use the observed structure to introduce conditional or hierarchical spaces rather than continuing with a flat parameter definition.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
text:
10.8 What Do Contour Plots Add?
Contour plots focus on pairwise interactions between parameters.
optuna.visualization.plot_contour(study)
They are especially informative when good performance is not attached to one parameter alone, but to a combination. A contour plot can reveal a valley, ridge, or island of strong performance that slice plots would hide.
Common diagnostic outcomes:

one parameter matters only when another is small
two parameters compensate for one another
a supposedly important parameter is important only because it interacts with another one

That is often the point where a search problem stops being “pick the right values” and becomes “represent the structure correctly.”

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.9 How Do Visuals Reveal a Bad Search Space?
text:
10.9 How Do Visuals Reveal a Bad Search Space?
Most failed studies are not failures of the sampler alone. They are failures of problem formulation.
Typical warning signs include:








Symptom
Likely issue
Typical fix




Best trials sit on parameter boundaries
Range too wide or too narrow
Re-center or rescale the space


Slice plots are flat
Parameter is weak or irrelevant
Remove it or reduce its emphasis


Good trials cluster logarithmically
Linear scale is inappropriate
Use log sampling


Convergence stalls early
Space is too broad or too noisy
Narrow the space or stabilize the objective



This is why visualization is not an afterthought. It is how you decide whether the next 200 trials will be useful or wasteful.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.10 How Do You Diagnose Parameter Interactions?
text:
10.10 How Do You Diagnose Parameter Interactions?
Interactions occur when the effect of one parameter depends on the value of another.
For example, a high learning rate may work only with a large batch size, while the same learning rate fails at smaller batches. If you ignore that interaction, you may incorrectly conclude that the learning rate itself is unstable.
The best tools for this are:

contour plots for pairwise structure
parallel coordinate plots for higher-dimensional patterns
parameter importance plots to see whether interaction-heavy parameters are globally influential

These signals often justify a redesign into conditional search spaces or alternative samplers, which connects directly back to Advanced Optimization Techniques and Samplers.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.11 When Is the Dashboard Worth Using?
text:
10.11 When Is the Dashboard Worth Using?
Static analysis is useful, but long-running or collaborative studies benefit from a live interface.
optuna-dashboard sqlite:///example.db
The dashboard is especially useful when you want:

live trial monitoring
filtering and comparing subsets of runs
interactive Pareto-front inspection for multi-objective studies
lightweight review of a shared experiment store

If you are moving from one-off studies to an experiment workflow, the dashboard is often the fastest route to operational visibility.






Common Mistake



Do not interpret diagnostic plots as ground truth when you have too few trials or a highly noisy objective. A parameter-importance ranking built from weak coverage can look precise while still being misleading.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
section: 10.12 Practical Exercise: Diagnose a Weak Study and Repair It
text:
10.12 Practical Exercise: Diagnose a Weak Study and Repair It
Use the study from the opening example as your baseline. Then generate the core diagnostics.
import optuna.visualization as vis

vis.plot_optimization_history(study)
vis.plot_param_importances(study)
vis.plot_slice(study)
vis.plot_parallel_coordinate(study)
vis.plot_contour(study)
Work through the exercise in four passes:

Inspect the optimization history and decide whether the study is still improving or mostly plateaued.
Use slice and importance plots to determine whether both x and y deserve the current range of [-100, 100].
Redefine the objective with a narrower range such as [-10, 10] and rerun the study with the same trial budget.
Compare the two studies and explain whether the redesign improved convergence, interpretability, or both.

Questions to answer after the run:

Did the incumbent improve earlier after narrowing the range?
Did parameter importance become easier to interpret?
Did the plots suggest a log scale, a narrower linear scale, or a conditional redesign?
If the objective remained noisy, would you spend more trials or reduce evaluation noise first?

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
text:
10.13 Recap
Diagnostics turn Optuna from a trial generator into an optimization workflow you can reason about.

Optimization history tells you whether progress is real, stalled, or hidden by noise.
Parameter-importance analysis helps prioritize which parameters deserve budget and attention.
Slice, contour, and parallel coordinate plots reveal boundaries, scaling mistakes, and interactions.
The dashboard is useful once studies become large, persistent, or collaborative.

title: 10  Visualization and Diagnostics
text:
10.14 Decision Guide
Use diagnostic tools as a redesign loop, not just as a report at the end.

Start with optimization history when you want to know whether the budget is still producing value.
Use slice plots when you suspect a parameter range or scale is poorly chosen.
Use contour and parallel coordinate plots when performance depends on interactions.
Use parameter importance to simplify the space, but only after checking that the study has enough coverage.
Use the dashboard when repeated or long-running studies need live visibility.

The next chapter extends this diagnostic mindset into infrastructure. In Distributed and Scalable Optimization, we will look at storage backends, parallel execution, and the operational concerns that appear once optimization leaves the single-process notebook setting.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
text:
11.1 Why This Matters
This chapter explains how to scale Optuna beyond the single-process workflow used in earlier modules. By the end, you should be able to choose between local parallelism and shared-storage distributed execution, understand the operational role of storage backends, and recognize the failure modes that appear once many workers collaborate on the same study.
Many real optimization problems are limited less by the quality of the sampler than by the cost of objective evaluation. If a single trial takes minutes or hours, sequential optimization quickly becomes the real bottleneck.
Scaling Optuna is therefore not an infrastructure afterthought. It is part of the optimization design itself. The storage backend, worker model, and failure handling strategy determine whether a large study is reproducible, efficient, and resumable.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.2 Fast First Win: Local Parallel Optimization
text:
11.2 Fast First Win: Local Parallel Optimization
The simplest form of scaling is to run several trials concurrently on one machine.

import math
import time

import optuna


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10.0, 10.0)
    time.sleep(0.02)
    return (x - 2.0) ** 2 + math.sin(5.0 * x)


study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=40, n_jobs=4)

print("Best value:", round(study.best_value, 4))
print("Best params:", study.best_params)
print("Completed trials:", len(study.trials))

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.3 What Does n_jobs Actually Buy You?
text:
11.3 What Does n_jobs Actually Buy You?
For many users, n_jobs is the first scaling lever:
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100, n_jobs=4)
Conceptually, the scheduler is simple:
Trial 1 -> worker slot 1
Trial 2 -> worker slot 2
Trial 3 -> worker slot 3
Trial 4 -> worker slot 4
This is attractive because it requires no extra infrastructure, but it has limits.

It is easiest when all workers live on one machine.
It does not solve cross-machine coordination.
CPU-bound Python workloads may not scale the same way as I/O-bound or external-process workloads.

So n_jobs is the right place to start, but not the right answer for every large experiment.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.4 When Do You Need Shared Storage?
text:
11.4 When Do You Need Shared Storage?
Once multiple processes or machines need to collaborate on the same study, Optuna needs persistent shared state. That state lives in the storage backend.
At that point, each worker follows the same loop:

Ask storage for the next trial.
Evaluate the objective.
Write the result back.
Request another trial.






flowchart LR
    A[Shared study storage] --> B[Worker requests trial]
    B --> C[Sampler proposes parameters]
    C --> D[Worker evaluates objective]
    D --> E[Result stored]
    E --> F[Next worker request]
    F --> A






The critical design shift is that storage is no longer just for persistence. It becomes the coordination mechanism for the whole experiment.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.5 Which Storage Backend Should You Use?
text:
11.5 Which Storage Backend Should You Use?
Optuna can work with several relational databases, but they are not interchangeable in practice.








Backend
Best fit
Main limitation




In-memory
quick local experiments
no persistence, no cross-process coordination


SQLite
small local shared studies
limited concurrency under heavier parallel load


PostgreSQL
serious distributed experiments
requires external service management


MySQL
distributed environments with existing MySQL ops
operational tuning still matters




11.5.1 In-Memory Storage

study = optuna.create_study(direction="minimize")


[I 2026-03-15 08:41:19,340] A new study created in memory with name: no-name-ed1084f2-9b92-4551-99d5-77ca8ff82542




This is ideal for exploratory work, but once the process exits, the study is gone.


11.5.2 SQLite Storage
study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    study_name="demo",
    storage="sqlite:///example.db",
    load_if_exists=True,
)
SQLite is a useful stepping stone for local experiments and demonstrations. It is not the backend you should automatically choose for many concurrent workers across serious production workloads.


11.5.3 PostgreSQL or MySQL
study = optuna.create_study(
    direction="minimize",
    study_name="experiment",
    storage="postgresql://user:pass@host/db",
    load_if_exists=True,
)
For real distributed optimization, PostgreSQL is often the safest default because it handles concurrency, transactions, and failure recovery more robustly than SQLite.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
text:
11.6 How Does Distributed Execution Work?
Distributed Optuna usually follows a producer-consumer pattern, but the important detail is that workers are asynchronous.
They do not advance in lockstep. Fast workers complete more trials. Slow workers may still be evaluating while others have already requested new configurations.
That means the system can keep hardware busy even when objective runtimes vary widely.
Create study
   -> worker requests trial
   -> sampler proposes parameters
   -> worker evaluates objective
   -> result written to storage
   -> next available worker requests another trial
Asynchronous behavior is usually an advantage, because synchronization barriers would waste compute. But it also means your study history reflects real system timing, not a clean round-based schedule.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
text:
11.7 What Happens When Workers Fail?
Distributed experiments are only useful if they survive crashes, interruptions, and restarts.
If a worker dies mid-trial, Optuna does not lose the whole study. Completed trials remain in storage, and other workers can continue. This is the operational reason storage matters so much.
Typical failure scenarios include:

a worker process crashes
a machine is preempted
a container restarts
a cluster job times out

With persistent storage, the study can be resumed instead of recomputed from scratch.
This is especially important in long-running workloads where trial budgets are expensive and experiments span multiple sessions.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.8 How Should You Think About Scalability?
text:
11.8 How Should You Think About Scalability?
There are different scaling regimes, and they imply different engineering choices.








Scale
Typical budget
Natural strategy




Small
50-200 trials
single process or n_jobs


Medium
hundreds to low thousands
multiple local or remote workers with persistent storage


Large
10,000+ trials
cluster execution with robust shared database and monitoring



The question is not simply “how many workers can I launch?” The real question is whether the objective function, storage backend, and monitoring stack can support that many workers without creating coordination or observability problems.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.9 What Does a Multi-Worker Setup Look Like?
text:
11.9 What Does a Multi-Worker Setup Look Like?
At the code level, all workers typically run the same script and point to the same study.

11.9.1 Step 1: Create or Load the Study
import optuna


study = optuna.create_study(
    study_name="distributed-demo",
    storage="sqlite:///example.db",
    load_if_exists=True,
    direction="minimize",
)


11.9.2 Step 2: Define the Worker Objective
def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10.0, 10.0)
    return (x - 2.0) ** 2


11.9.3 Step 3: Run the Worker
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=100)
Launch the same worker script multiple times:
python worker.py
python worker.py
python worker.py
All workers collaborate through the same storage backend. That is the core distributed pattern.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.10 How Do You Monitor a Large Study?
text:
11.10 How Do You Monitor a Large Study?
Once experiments become large, you need visibility while the optimization is still running.
Programmatic monitoring is the minimum level:
print("Best value:", study.best_value)
print("Completed trials:", len(study.trials))
For interactive monitoring, the dashboard is more useful:
optuna-dashboard sqlite:///example.db
This is especially helpful when you want to inspect trial progress, compare parameter regions, or review multi-objective frontiers without writing custom reporting code.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.11 How Does Optuna Fit Into Larger Distributed Systems?
text:
11.11 How Does Optuna Fit Into Larger Distributed Systems?
Optuna is often not the orchestrator itself. Instead, it is the optimization layer inside a broader execution environment.
Common combinations include:



System
Typical role




Ray
distributed ML workloads and task execution


Dask
parallel Python workflows


Kubernetes
container orchestration for scalable workers


SLURM
batch scheduling on HPC clusters



The pattern is usually the same:

the external platform launches workers
Optuna storage coordinates the study
workers report results back into the shared study state

That separation of concerns is useful. Optuna handles optimization logic, while the surrounding platform handles resource scheduling.






Common Mistake



Do not assume SQLite is a production-grade answer for every distributed setup just because it works for a small demo. Once concurrency, fault tolerance, and worker count increase, backend limitations become part of the optimization bottleneck.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
section: 11.12 Practical Exercise: Turn a Local Study Into a Shared Study
text:
11.12 Practical Exercise: Turn a Local Study Into a Shared Study
Start from a simple single-process optimization script and upgrade it into a shared-storage workflow.
Use this objective:

import math

import optuna


def objective(trial):
    x = trial.suggest_float("x", -10.0, 10.0)
    return (x - 2.0) ** 2 + math.sin(5.0 * x)

Then work through the exercise in four passes:

Run the study in a single process and record the best value after 50 trials.
Run it again with n_jobs=4 and compare wall-clock behavior.
Move the study to shared SQLite storage using load_if_exists=True and launch the worker script from multiple terminals.
Replace SQLite with PostgreSQL conceptually and list what operational risks improve when you move to a stronger backend.

Questions to answer after the run:

Which part of the speedup came from concurrency and which part was still limited by the objective?
Did local parallelism solve the problem, or did you still need shared storage for resumability?
At what point would SQLite stop being a comfortable choice?
If your workers lived on Kubernetes, Ray, or SLURM, what would Optuna handle and what would the platform handle?

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
text:
11.13 Recap
Distributed Optuna is fundamentally about coordination, not just raw worker count.

n_jobs provides the simplest local concurrency.
Shared storage is required once multiple processes or machines collaborate on the same study.
SQLite is useful for small shared experiments, while PostgreSQL or MySQL are better suited to serious distributed workloads.
Persistent storage enables fault tolerance, resumability, and monitoring.

title: 11  Distributed and Scalable Optimization
text:
11.14 Decision Guide
Choose the simplest scaling model that matches the workload.

Use a single process for cheap exploratory studies.
Use n_jobs when one machine is enough and concurrency is the main need.
Use shared storage when workers must coordinate across processes or hosts.
Prefer PostgreSQL-class backends when the study is important enough that failures, locking, and concurrency behavior matter.
Use external platforms such as Ray, Dask, Kubernetes, or SLURM for resource orchestration, while letting Optuna remain the optimization layer.

The next chapter moves from infrastructure back to applications. In Optuna for Machine Learning, we will apply the ideas from this book to realistic model-selection and pipeline-tuning workflows.

title: 12  Optuna for Machine Learning
text:
12.1 Why This Matters
This chapter applies Optuna to real machine-learning workflows rather than toy functions. By the end, you should be able to structure an Optuna study around robust model evaluation, tune scikit-learn pipelines safely, understand how Optuna integrates with major ML frameworks, and avoid the most common evaluation errors such as leakage and validation overfitting.
In machine learning, a good optimizer is not enough. If the evaluation loop is flawed, Optuna will simply optimize the wrong target more efficiently.
That is why ML optimization is a distinct topic. The hard part is often not parameter suggestion itself, but building an objective function that reflects generalization performance, respects data boundaries, and remains reproducible across runs.

title: 12  Optuna for Machine Learning
section: 12.2 Fast First Win: Tune a Real Classifier Safely
text:
12.2 Fast First Win: Tune a Real Classifier Safely
Start with a compact but realistic workflow: a scikit-learn pipeline evaluated with cross-validation on a real dataset.

import optuna
from sklearn.datasets import load_breast_cancer
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler


X, y = load_breast_cancer(return_X_y=True)


def objective(trial):
    n_estimators = trial.suggest_int("n_estimators", 50, 250)
    max_depth = trial.suggest_int("max_depth", 2, 16)
    min_samples_split = trial.suggest_int("min_samples_split", 2, 10)

    model = Pipeline(
        [
            ("scaler", StandardScaler()),
            (
                "clf",
                RandomForestClassifier(
                    n_estimators=n_estimators,
                    max_depth=max_depth,
                    min_samples_split=min_samples_split,
                    random_state=42,
                ),
            ),
        ]
    )

    scores = cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5, scoring="accuracy")
    return scores.mean()


sampler = optuna.samplers.TPESampler(seed=42)
study = optuna.create_study(direction="maximize", sampler=sampler)
study.optimize(objective, n_trials=30)

print("Best score:", round(study.best_value, 4))
print("Best params:", study.best_params)
